[Low German](/wiki/Low_German "Low German"), [Polish](/wiki/Polish_language "Polish language"), [Danish](/wiki/Danish_language "Danish language"), [French](/wiki/French_language "French language"), [Yiddish](/wiki/Yiddish "Yiddish"), [Limburgish](/wiki/Limburgish_language "Limburgish language"), [Frisian](/wiki/Frisian_languages "Frisian languages"), [Sorbian](/wiki/Sorbian_languages "Sorbian languages"), [Sinte Romani](/wiki/Sinte_Romani "Sinte Romani"), [Lithuanian](/wiki/Lithuanian_language "Lithuanian language"), [Kashubian](/wiki/Kashubian_language "Kashubian language"), [Swahili](/wiki/Swahili_language "Swahili language"), [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic "Arabic") |

| **Succeeded by** | | |  |  | | --- | --- | |  | [North German Confederation](/wiki/North_German_Confederation "North German Confederation") | |  | [Bavaria](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Bavaria "Kingdom of Bavaria") | |  | [Württemberg](/wiki/Kingdom_of_W%C3%BCrttemberg "Kingdom of Württemberg") | |  | [Baden](/wiki/Grand_Duchy_of_Baden "Grand Duchy of Baden") | |  | [Hesse](/wiki/Grand_Duchy_of_Hesse "Grand Duchy of Hesse") | | |  |  | | --- | --- | | [Weimar Republic](/wiki/Weimar_Republic "Weimar Republic") |  | | [People's State of Bavaria](/wiki/People%27s_State_of_Bavaria "People's State of Bavaria") |  | | [Alsace-Lorraine Soviet Republic](/wiki/Alsace-Lorraine_Soviet_Republic "Alsace-Lorraine Soviet Republic") |  | | [Bremen Soviet Republic](/wiki/Bremen_Soviet_Republic "Bremen Soviet Republic") |  | | [Mainz Workers' and Soldiers' Council](/wiki/Mainz_Workers%27_and_Soldiers%27_Council "Mainz Workers' and Soldiers' Council") |  | | | |

([German](/wiki/German_language "German language"): *Deutsches Reich*),[[a]](#cite_note-10)[[10]](#cite_note-constitution-11)[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhitaker1990765-12) also referred to as **Imperial Germany**,[[12]](#cite_note-13) the **Second Reich**,[[b]](#cite_note-14)[[13]](#cite_note-15) or simply **Germany**, was the period of the [German Reich](/wiki/German_Reich "German Reich")[[14]](#cite_note-CNMP-H-78-KY-M-20230413-16) from the [unification of Germany](/wiki/Unification_of_Germany "Unification of Germany") in 1871 until the [November Revolution in 1918](/wiki/German_revolution_of_1918%E2%80%931919 "German revolution of 1918–1919"), when Germany changed its form of government to [a republic](/wiki/Weimar_Republic "Weimar Republic").[[15]](#cite_note-17) The German Empire consisted of [25 states](/wiki/States_of_the_German_Empire "States of the German Empire"), each with its own [nobility](/wiki/Nobility "Nobility"): four constituent [kingdoms](/wiki/Monarchy "Monarchy"), six [grand duchies](/wiki/Grand_duchy "Grand duchy"), five [duchies](/wiki/Duchy "Duchy") (six before 1876), seven [principalities](/wiki/Principality "Principality"), three [free](/wiki/Free_imperial_city "Free imperial city") [Hanseatic](/wiki/Hanseatic_League "Hanseatic League") [cities](/wiki/City-state "City-state"), and [one imperial territory](/wiki/Alsace%E2%80%93Lorraine "Alsace–Lorraine"). While [Prussia](/wiki/Prussia "Prussia") was only one of the four kingdoms in the realm, it contained about two-thirds of the Empire's population and territory, and Prussian dominance was also constitutionally established, since the King of [Prussia](/wiki/Prussia "Prussia") was also the German Emperor (*Deutscher Kaiser*).

The **German Empire** ([German](/wiki/German_language "German language"): *Deutsches Reich*),[[a]](#cite_note-10)[[10]](#cite_note-constitution-11)[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhitaker1990765-12) also referred to as **Imperial Germany**,[[12]](#cite_note-13) the **Second Reich**,[[b]](#cite_note-14)[[13]](#cite_note-15) or simply **Germany**, was the period of the [German Reich](/wiki/German_Reich "German Reich")[[14]](#cite_note-CNMP-H-78-KY-M-20230413-16) from the [unification of Germany](/wiki/Unification_of_Germany "Unification of Germany") in 1871 until the [November Revolution in 1918](/wiki/German_revolution_of_1918%E2%80%931919 "German revolution of 1918–1919"), when Germany changed its form of government to [a republic](/wiki/Weimar_Republic "Weimar Republic").[[15]](#cite_note-17) The German Empire consisted of [25 states](/wiki/States_of_the_German_Empire "States of the German Empire"), each with its own [nobility](/wiki/Nobility "Nobility"): four constituent [kingdoms](/wiki/Monarchy "Monarchy"), six [grand duchies](/wiki/Grand_duchy "Grand duchy"), five [duchies](/wiki/Duchy "Duchy") (six before 1876), seven [principalities](/wiki/Principality "Principality"), three [free](/wiki/Free_imperial_city "Free imperial city") [Hanseatic](/wiki/Hanseatic_League "Hanseatic League") [cities](/wiki/City-state "City-state"), and [one imperial territory](/wiki/Alsace%E2%80%93Lorraine "Alsace–Lorraine"). While [Prussia](/wiki/Prussia "Prussia") was only one of the four kingdoms in the realm, it contained about two-thirds of the Empire's population and territory, and Prussian dominance was also constitutionally established, since the King of [Prussia](/wiki/Prussia "Prussia") was also the German Emperor (*Deutscher Kaiser*).

The empire was [founded](/wiki/Proclamation_of_the_German_Empire "Proclamation of the German Empire") on 18 January 1871, when the south German states, except for [Austria](/wiki/Cisleithania "Cisleithania"), [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland "Switzerland") and [Liechtenstein](/wiki/Liechtenstein "Liechtenstein"), joined the [North German Confederation](/wiki/North_German_Confederation "North German Confederation"). The [new constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_the_German_Empire "Constitution of the German Empire") came into force on 16 April, changing the name of the federal state to the German Empire and introducing the title of [German Emperor](/wiki/German_Emperor "German Emperor") for [Wilhelm I](/wiki/William_I,_German_Emperor "William I, German Emperor"), [King of Prussia](/wiki/List_of_monarchs_of_Prussia "List of monarchs of Prussia") from the [House of Hohenzollern](/wiki/House_of_Hohenzollern "House of Hohenzollern").[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKotulla2006231,_246-18) [Berlin](/wiki/Berlin "Berlin") remained its capital, and [Otto von Bismarck](/wiki/Otto_von_Bismarck "Otto von Bismarck"), [Minister President of Prussia](/wiki/Minister_President_of_Prussia "Minister President of Prussia"), became [chancellor](/wiki/Chancellor_of_Germany "Chancellor of Germany"), the head of government. During its 47 years of existence, the German Empire became an industrial, technological, and scientific power in Europe, and by 1913, Germany was the [largest economy](/wiki/Angus_Maddison_statistics_of_the_ten_largest_economies_by_GDP_(PPP) "Angus Maddison statistics of the ten largest economies by GDP (PPP)") in continental Europe and the third largest in the world.[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGat2008517-19) The success of the German Empire in the natural sciences was such that one-third of all [Nobel Prizes](/wiki/Nobel_Prize "Nobel Prize") went to German inventors and researchers. Germany also became a [great power](/wiki/Great_power "Great power"), building the longest railway network of Europe, the world's strongest army,[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVagts1939210–221-20) and a fast-growing industrial base.[[19]](#cite_note-21) Starting very small in 1871, in a decade, the [navy](/wiki/Imperial_German_Navy "Imperial German Navy") became second only to [Britain](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland "United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland")'s [Royal Navy](/wiki/Royal_Navy "Royal Navy").

Otto von Bismarck served as the first and longest-tenured chancellor of the German Empire from 1871 to 1890. His tenure began with relatively liberal measures and broad reforms but gradually shifted toward conservatism, marked by the *[Kulturkampf](/wiki/Kulturkampf "Kulturkampf")* against the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church"). In foreign affairs, Bismarck concluded the [Dual Alliance](/wiki/Dual_Alliance_(1879) "Dual Alliance (1879)") with [Austria-Hungary](/wiki/Austria-Hungary "Austria-Hungary") in 1879, expanded into the [Triple Alliance](/wiki/Triple_Alliance_(1882) "Triple Alliance (1882)") with [Italy](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Italy "Kingdom of Italy") in 1882, while also fostering close ties to the [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire"). Despite denouncing [liberals](/wiki/Liberalism "Liberalism") and [socialists](/wiki/Socialism "Socialism") as "enemies of the Reich", he introduced pioneering [social programs](/wiki/State_Socialism_(Germany) "State Socialism (Germany)") that laid the foundation for the modern European [welfare state](/wiki/Welfare_state "Welfare state"). In the 1880s, Germany entered the [colonial race](/wiki/Scramble_for_Africa "Scramble for Africa") despite Bismarck's earlier reluctance, acquiring territories in Africa, the Pacific, and China and building [the world's third-largest colonial empire](/wiki/German_colonial_empire "German colonial empire") after the [British](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire") and [French](/wiki/French_colonial_empire "French colonial empire").[[20]](#cite_note-22) Following his dismissal in 1890, [Wilhelm II](/wiki/Wilhelm_II,_German_Emperor "Wilhelm II, German Emperor") pursued *[Weltpolitik](/wiki/Weltpolitik "Weltpolitik")* ("world politics"), a more aggressive and expansionist course that abandoned Bismarck's complex alliance system, leaving Germany increasingly isolated. When the [July Crisis](/wiki/July_Crisis "July Crisis") of 1914 escalated into the [First World War](/wiki/First_World_War "First World War"), Italy distanced itself from the Triple Alliance while the Ottoman Empire [aligned with Germany](/wiki/German%E2%80%93Ottoman_alliance "German–Ottoman alliance"). The emperor's inconsistent and often unpredictable decisions contributed to the tensions that culminated in the outbreak of the war.

In the First World War, [German plans to capture Paris quickly](/wiki/Schlieffen_Plan "Schlieffen Plan") in the autumn of 1914 failed, and the war on the [Western Front](/wiki/Western_Front_(World_War_I) "Western Front (World War I)") became a stalemate. The [Allied naval blockade](/wiki/Blockade_of_Germany_(1914%E2%80%931919) "Blockade of Germany (1914–1919)") caused severe shortages of food and supplements. However, Imperial Germany had success on the [Eastern Front](/wiki/Eastern_Front_(World_War_I) "Eastern Front (World War I)"); it occupied a large amount of territory to its east following the [Treaty of Brest-Litovsk](/wiki/Treaty_of_Brest-Litovsk "Treaty of Brest-Litovsk"). The German declaration of [unrestricted submarine warfare](/wiki/Unrestricted_submarine_warfare "Unrestricted submarine warfare") in early 1917 contributed to bringing the United States into the war. In October 1918, after the failed [Spring Offensive](/wiki/German_spring_offensive "German spring offensive"), the German armies [were in retreat](/wiki/Hundred_Days_Offensive "Hundred Days Offensive"), allies Austria-Hungary and the Ottoman Empire had collapsed, and [Bulgaria](/wiki/Bulgaria_during_World_War_I "Bulgaria during World War I") had surrendered. The empire collapsed in the [November 1918 Revolution](/wiki/German_Revolution_of_1918%E2%80%931919 "German Revolution of 1918–1919") with the abdication of Wilhelm II, which left the post-war federal republic to govern a devastated populace. The [Treaty of Versailles](/wiki/Treaty_of_Versailles "Treaty of Versailles") imposed post-war reparation costs of 132 billion [gold marks](/wiki/German_mark_(1871) "German mark (1871)") (around US$269 billion or €240 billion in 2019, or roughly US$32 billion in 1921),[[21]](#cite_note-23) as well as limiting the army to 100,000 men and disallowing conscription, armored vehicles, submarines, aircraft, and more than six battleships.[[22]](#cite_note-24) The consequential economic devastation, later exacerbated by the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression"), as well as humiliation and outrage experienced by the German population are considered leading factors in the [rise of Adolf Hitler](/wiki/Rise_of_Adolf_Hitler "Rise of Adolf Hitler") and [Nazism](/wiki/Nazism "Nazism").[[23]](#cite_note-25)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=German_Empire&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Background

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=German_Empire&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Background")]

Main article: [Unification of Germany](/wiki/Unification_of_Germany "Unification of Germany")

Founders of the German Empire

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e5/Otto_von_Bismarck.JPG/250px-Otto_von_Bismarck.JPG)](/wiki/File:Otto_von_Bismarck.JPG)

Chancellor [Otto von Bismarck](/wiki/Otto_von_Bismarck "Otto von Bismarck") (pictured in 1873)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7f/Wilhelm_I_German_Emperor_circa_1870.jpg/250px-Wilhelm_I_German_Emperor_circa_1870.jpg)](/wiki/File:Wilhelm_I_German_Emperor_circa_1870.jpg)

Emperor [Wilhelm I](/wiki/William_I,_German_Emperor "William I, German Emperor") (pictured c. 1870)

The [German Confederation](/wiki/German_Confederation "German Confederation") had been created by an act of the [Congress of Vienna](/wiki/Congress_of_Vienna "Congress of Vienna") on 8 June 1815 as a result of the [Napoleonic Wars](/wiki/Napoleonic_Wars "Napoleonic Wars"), after being alluded to in [Article 6](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Treaty_of_Paris_(1814)#ART.VI "wikisource:Treaty of Paris (1814)") of the 1814 [Treaty of Paris](/wiki/Treaty_of_Paris_(1814) "Treaty of Paris (1814)").[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeeren1873480-26)

The liberal [Revolutions of 1848](/wiki/Revolutions_of_1848 "Revolutions of 1848") were crushed after the relations between the educated, well-off middle-class liberals and the urban artisans broke down; [Otto von Bismarck](/wiki/Otto_von_Bismarck "Otto von Bismarck")'s pragmatic *[Realpolitik](/wiki/Realpolitik "Realpolitik")*, which appealed to peasants as well as the aristocracy, took its place.[[25]](#cite_note-27) Bismarck sought to extend [Hohenzollern](/wiki/House_of_Hohenzollern "House of Hohenzollern") hegemony throughout the German states; to do so meant unification of the German states and the exclusion of [Prussia](/wiki/Prussia "Prussia")'s main German rival, [Austria](/wiki/Austrian_Empire "Austrian Empire"), from the subsequent German Empire. He envisioned a conservative, Prussian-dominated Germany. The [Second Schleswig War](/wiki/Second_Schleswig_War "Second Schleswig War") against [Denmark](/wiki/Denmark "Denmark") in 1864, the [Austro-Prussian War](/wiki/Austro-Prussian_War "Austro-Prussian War") in 1866, and the [Franco-Prussian War](/wiki/Franco-Prussian_War "Franco-Prussian War") in 1870–1871 sparked a growing pan-German ideal and contributed to the formation of the German state, with victory to the *[Kleindeutsche Lösung](/wiki/Kleindeutsche_L%C3%B6sung "Kleindeutsche Lösung")* ("[Lesser German](/wiki/Lesser_Germany "Lesser Germany") solution").[[26]](#cite_note-28)[[27]](#cite_note-29)

The [German Confederation](/wiki/German_Confederation "German Confederation") ended as a result of the Austro-Prussian War of 1866 between the constituent Confederation entities of the [Austrian Empire](/wiki/Austrian_Empire "Austrian Empire") and its allies on one side and Prussia and its allies on the other. The war resulted in the partial replacement of the Confederation in 1867 by a [North German Confederation](/wiki/North_German_Confederation "North German Confederation"), comprising the 22 states north of the river [Main](/wiki/Main_(river) "Main (river)"). The patriotic fervor generated by the Franco-Prussian War of 1870 overwhelmed the remaining opposition to a unified Germany (aside from Austria) in the four states south of the Main, and during November 1870, they joined the North German Confederation by treaty.[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECase1902139-30)

#### Foundation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=German_Empire&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Foundation")]

Main article: [Proclamation of the German Empire](/wiki/Proclamation_of_the_German_Empire "Proclamation of the German Empire")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/72/A_v_Werner_-_Kaiserproklamation_am_18_Januar_1871_%283._Fassung_1885%29.jpg/250px-A_v_Werner_-_Kaiserproklamation_am_18_Januar_1871_%283._Fassung_1885%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:A_v_Werner_-_Kaiserproklamation_am_18_Januar_1871_(3._Fassung_1885).jpg)

*Die Proklamation des Deutschen Kaiserreiches* by [Anton von Werner](/wiki/Anton_von_Werner "Anton von Werner") (1877), depicting the proclamation of Emperor William I (18 January 1871, [Palace of Versailles](/wiki/Palace_of_Versailles "Palace of Versailles")). From left, on the podium (in black): Crown Prince Frederick (later [Frederick III](/wiki/Frederick_III,_German_Emperor "Frederick III, German Emperor")), his father the emperor, and [Frederick I of Baden](/wiki/Frederick_I,_Grand_Duke_of_Baden "Frederick I, Grand Duke of Baden"), proposing a toast to the new emperor. At centre (in white): Otto von Bismarck, first Chancellor of Germany, [Helmuth von Moltke the Elder](/wiki/Helmuth_von_Moltke_the_Elder "Helmuth von Moltke the Elder"), Prussian Chief of Staff.

On 10 December 1870, the North German Confederation Reichstag renamed the Confederation the "German Empire" and gave the title of [German Emperor](/wiki/German_Emperor "German Emperor") to [William I](/wiki/William_I,_German_Emperor "William I, German Emperor"), the [King of Prussia](/wiki/King_of_Prussia "King of Prussia"), as *Bundespräsidium* of the Confederation.[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECase1902139–140-31) The new constitution ([Constitution of the German Confederation](/wiki/Constitution_of_the_German_Confederation_(1871) "Constitution of the German Confederation (1871)")) and the title Emperor came into effect on 1 January 1871. During the [siege of Paris](/wiki/Siege_of_Paris_(1870%E2%80%931871) "Siege of Paris (1870–1871)") on 18 January 1871, William was proclaimed Emperor in the [Hall of Mirrors](/wiki/Hall_of_Mirrors "Hall of Mirrors") at the [Palace of Versailles](/wiki/Palace_of_Versailles "Palace of Versailles").[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECase1902140-32)

The second [German Constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_the_German_Empire "Constitution of the German Empire"), adopted by the Reichstag on 14 April 1871 and proclaimed by the Emperor on 16 April,[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECase1902140-32) was substantially based upon Bismarck's [North German Constitution](/wiki/North_German_Constitution "North German Constitution"). The political system remained the same. The empire had a parliament called the *[Reichstag](/wiki/Reichstag_(German_Empire) "Reichstag (German Empire)")*, which was elected by [universal male suffrage](/wiki/Universal_suffrage "Universal suffrage"). However, the original constituencies drawn in 1871 were never redrawn to reflect the growth of urban areas. As a result, by the time of the great expansion of German cities in the 1890s and 1900s, rural areas were grossly [over-represented](/wiki/Malapportionment "Malapportionment").

Legislation also required the consent of the *[Bundesrat](/wiki/Bundesrat_(German_Empire) "Bundesrat (German Empire)")*, the federal council of deputies from the 25 states.[[31]](#cite_note-33) Executive power was vested in the emperor, or *[Kaiser](/wiki/Kaiser "Kaiser")*, who was assisted by a [chancellor](/wiki/Chancellor_of_Germany "Chancellor of Germany") responsible only to him. The emperor was given extensive powers by the constitution. He alone appointed and dismissed the chancellor (so in practice, the emperor ruled the empire through the chancellor), was supreme commander-in-chief of the armed forces, and final arbiter of all foreign affairs, and could also disband the *Reichstag* to call for new elections. Officially, the chancellor was a one-man cabinet and was responsible for the conduct of all state affairs; in practice, the [State Secretaries](/wiki/State_Secretary "State Secretary") (top bureaucratic officials in charge of such fields as finance, war, foreign affairs, etc.) functioned much like ministers in other monarchies. The *Reichstag* had the power to pass, amend, or reject bills and to initiate legislation. However, as mentioned above, in practice, the real power was vested in the emperor, who exercised it through his chancellor.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4f/Berlin_Nationaldenkmal_Kaiser_Wilhelm_mit_Schloss_1900.jpg/250px-Berlin_Nationaldenkmal_Kaiser_Wilhelm_mit_Schloss_1900.jpg)](/wiki/File:Berlin_Nationaldenkmal_Kaiser_Wilhelm_mit_Schloss_1900.jpg)

[Berlin Palace](/wiki/Berlin_Palace "Berlin Palace"), the main residence of the House of Hohenzollern

Although nominally a federal empire and league of equals, in practice, the empire was dominated by the largest and most powerful state, Prussia. It stretched across the northern two-thirds of the new *Reich* and contained three-fifths of the country's population. The imperial crown was hereditary in the ruling house of Prussia, the [House of Hohenzollern](/wiki/House_of_Hohenzollern "House of Hohenzollern"). With the exception of 1872–1873 and 1892–1894, the chancellor was always simultaneously the prime minister of Prussia. With 17 out of 58 votes in the *Bundesrat*, Berlin needed only a few votes from the smaller states to exercise effective control.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The other states retained their own governments but had only limited aspects of sovereignty. For example, both postage stamps and currency were issued for the empire as a whole. Coins through one mark were also minted in the name of the empire, while higher-valued pieces were issued by the states. However, these larger gold and silver issues were virtually [commemorative coins](/wiki/Commemorative_coin "Commemorative coin") and had limited circulation. While the states issued their own [decorations](/wiki/Military_awards_and_decorations "Military awards and decorations") and some had their own armies, the military forces of the smaller ones were put under Prussian control. Those of the larger states, such as the Kingdoms of Bavaria and Saxony, were coordinated along Prussian principles and would, in wartime, be controlled by the federal government. The evolution of the German Empire is somewhat in line with parallel developments in Italy, which became a united nation-state a decade earlier. Some key elements of the German Empire's authoritarian political structure were also the basis for conservative modernization in [Imperial Japan](/wiki/Empire_of_Japan "Empire of Japan") under [Emperor Meiji](/wiki/Emperor_Meiji "Emperor Meiji") and the preservation of an authoritarian political structure under the [tsars](/wiki/Tsar "Tsar") in the [Russian Empire](/wiki/Russian_Empire "Russian Empire").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

One factor in the social anatomy of these governments was the retention of a very substantial share in political power by the [landed elite](/wiki/Landed_elite "Landed elite"), the [Junkers](/wiki/Junker "Junker"), resulting from the absence of a revolutionary breakthrough by the peasants in combination with urban areas.

Although authoritarian in many respects, the empire had some democratic features. Besides universal manhood suffrage, it permitted the development of political parties. Bismarck intended to create a constitutional façade that would mask the continuation of authoritarian policies. However, in the process, he created a system with a serious flaw. There was a significant disparity between the Prussian and German electoral systems. Prussia used a [three-class voting system](/wiki/Prussian_three-class_franchise "Prussian three-class franchise") which weighted votes based on the amount of taxes paid,[[32]](#cite_note-34) all but assuring a conservative majority. The king and (with two exceptions) the prime minister of Prussia were also the emperor and chancellor of the empire – meaning that the same rulers had to seek majorities from legislatures elected from completely different franchises. Universal suffrage was significantly diluted by gross over-representation of rural areas from the 1890s onward. From 1871 to the turn of the century, the urban-rural population balance shifted significantly, going from 64% rural and 36% urban in 1871 to 46% rural and 54% urban in 1900.[[33]](#cite_note-35)

Bismarck's domestic policies played an important role in forging the authoritarian political culture of the *Kaiserreich*. Less preoccupied with continental power politics following unification in 1871, Germany's semi-parliamentary government carried out a relatively smooth economic and political revolution from above that pushed them along the way towards becoming the world's leading industrial power of the time.

Bismarck's "revolutionary conservatism" was a conservative state-building strategy designed to make ordinary Germans—not just the Junker elite—more loyal to the throne and empire. According to Kees van Kersbergen and Barbara Vis, his strategy was:

> granting social rights to enhance the integration of a hierarchical society, to forge a bond between workers and the state so as to strengthen the latter, to maintain traditional relations of authority between social and status groups, and to provide a countervailing power against the modernist forces of liberalism and socialism.[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKersbergenVis201338-36)

Bismarck created the modern welfare state in Germany in the 1880s and enacted universal male suffrage in 1871.[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMooreVaudagna2003226-37) He became a great hero to German conservatives, who erected many monuments to his memory and tried to emulate his policies.[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFrankel2003543–560-38)

#### Foreign policy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=German_Empire&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Foreign policy")]

Further information: [International relations (1814–1919)](/wiki/International_relations_(1814%E2%80%931919) "International relations (1814–1919)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/7/78/Karolinen-stamp.jpg)](/wiki/File:Karolinen-stamp.jpg)

A postage stamp from the [Caroline Islands](/wiki/Caroline_Islands "Caroline Islands")

Bismarck's post-1871 foreign policy was conservative and sought to preserve the balance of power in Europe. British historian [Eric Hobsbawm](/wiki/Eric_Hobsbawm "Eric Hobsbawm") concludes that he "remained undisputed world champion at the game of multilateral diplomatic chess for almost twenty years after 1871, [devoting] himself exclusively, and successfully, to maintaining peace between the powers".[[37]](#cite_note-39) This was a departure from his adventurous foreign policy for Prussia, where he favored strength and expansion, punctuating this by saying, "The great questions of the age are not settled by speeches and majority votes – this was the error of 1848–49 – but by iron and blood."[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHolborn1982162-40)

Bismarck's chief concern was that France would plot revenge after its defeat in the [Franco-Prussian War](/wiki/Franco-Prussian_War "Franco-Prussian War"). As the French lacked the strength to defeat Germany by themselves, they sought an alliance with Russia, or perhaps even the newly reformed empire of Austria-Hungary, which would envelope Germany completely. Bismarck wanted to prevent this at all costs and maintain friendly relations with the Austrians and the Russians, signing the [Dual Alliance (1879)](/wiki/Dual_Alliance_(1879) "Dual Alliance (1879)") with Austria-Hungary in 1879. The Dual Alliance was a defensive alliance that was established against Russia, and by association France, in the event alliance did not work out with the state. However, an alliance with Russia would come not long after the signing of the Dual Alliance with Austria, the *[Dreikaiserbund](/wiki/League_of_the_Three_Emperors "League of the Three Emperors")* (League of Three Emperors), in 1881. During this period, individuals within the German military were advocating a preemptive strike against Russia, but Bismarck knew that such ideas were foolhardy. He once wrote that "the most brilliant victories would not avail against the Russian nation, because of its climate, its desert, and its frugality, and having but one frontier to defend", and because it would leave Germany with another bitter, resentful neighbor. Despite this, another alliance between Germany, Austria-Hungary, and Italy would be signed in 1882, preying on the fears of the German and Austro-Hungarian militaries of the untrustworthiness of Russia itself. This [Triple Alliance](/wiki/Triple_Alliance_(1882) "Triple Alliance (1882)") continued to 1915, when Italy declared war on Austria-Hungary. Despite Germany, and especially Austria's, lack of faith in the Russian alliance, the [Reinsurance Treaty](/wiki/Reinsurance_Treaty#:~:text=The_Reinsurance_Treaty_was_a,Petersburg. "Reinsurance Treaty") would be first signed in 1887, and renewed up until 1890, when the Bismarckian system collapsed upon Bismarck's resignation.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Meanwhile, the chancellor remained wary of any foreign policy developments that looked even remotely warlike. In 1886, he moved to stop an attempted sale of horses to France because they might be used for cavalry and also ordered an investigation into large Russian purchases of medicine from a German chemical works. Bismarck stubbornly refused to listen to [Georg Herbert Münster](/wiki/Georg_Herbert_M%C3%BCnster "Georg Herbert Münster"), ambassador to France, who reported back that the French were not seeking a revanchist war and were desperate for peace at all costs.
Bismarck and most of his contemporaries were conservative-minded and focused their foreign policy attention on Germany's neighboring states. In 1914, 60% of German foreign investment was in Europe, as opposed to just 5% of British investment. Most of the money went to developing nations such as Russia that lacked the capital or technical knowledge to industrialize on their own. The construction of the [Berlin–Baghdad railway](/wiki/Berlin%E2%80%93Baghdad_railway "Berlin–Baghdad railway"), financed by German banks, was designed to eventually connect Germany with the Ottoman Empire and the [Persian Gulf](/wiki/Persian_Gulf "Persian Gulf"), but it also collided with British and Russian geopolitical interests. Conflict over the Baghdad Railway was resolved in June 1914.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Many consider Bismarck's foreign policy as a coherent system and partly responsible for the preservation of Europe's stability. It was also marked by the need to balance circumspect defensiveness and the desire to be free from the constraints of its position as a major European power. [[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETipton2003170-41) Bismarck's successors did not pursue his foreign policy legacy. For instance, Kaiser Wilhelm II, who dismissed the chancellor in 1890, let the treaty with Russia lapse in favor of Germany's alliance with Austria, which finally led to a stronger coalition-building between Russia and France.[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGvosdevMarsh2013241-42)

#### Colonies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=German_Empire&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Colonies")]

Main article: [German colonial empire](/wiki/German_colonial_empire "German colonial empire")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8c/German_colonial.PNG/500px-German_colonial.PNG)](/wiki/File:German_colonial.PNG)

The [German colonial empire](/wiki/German_colonial_empire "German colonial empire") and its protectorates in 1914

Germans had dreamed of colonial imperialism since 1848.[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFitzpatrick2007135–161-43) Although Bismarck had little interest in acquiring overseas possessions, most Germans were enthusiastic, and by 1884 he had acquired [German New Guinea](/wiki/German_New_Guinea "German New Guinea").[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECiarlo2008285–298-44) By the 1890s, German colonial expansion in Asia and the Pacific ([Jiaozhou Bay](/wiki/Jiaozhou_Bay "Jiaozhou Bay") and [Tianjin](/wiki/Tianjin "Tianjin") in China, the [Marianas](/wiki/Mariana_Islands "Mariana Islands"), the [Caroline Islands](/wiki/Caroline_Islands "Caroline Islands"), Samoa) led to frictions with the UK, Russia, Japan, and the US. The largest colonial enterprises were in Africa,[[43]](#cite_note-45)[[44]](#cite_note-46) where the [Herero Wars](/wiki/Herero_Wars "Herero Wars") in what is now [Namibia](/wiki/Namibia "Namibia") in 1906–1907 resulted in the [Herero and Nama genocide](/wiki/Herero_and_Nama_genocide "Herero and Nama genocide").[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDedering199380–88-47)

#### Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=German_Empire&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Economy")]

Further information: [Economic history of Germany](/wiki/Economic_history_of_Germany "Economic history of Germany")

See also: [Urbanization in the German Empire](/wiki/Urbanization_in_the_German_Empire "Urbanization in the German Empire")

By 1900, Germany became the largest economy in continental Europe and the third largest in the world behind the United States and the [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire"), which were also its main economic rivals. Throughout its existence, it experienced economic growth and modernization led by heavy industry. In 1871, it had a largely rural population of 41 million, while by 1913, this had increased to a predominantly urban population of 68 million.[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMilwardSaul197717–70-48)

##### Industrial power

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=German_Empire&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Industrial power")]

Main articles: [Economic history of Germany § Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Economic_history_of_Germany#Industrial_Revolution "Economic history of Germany"), and [Industrialization in Germany](/wiki/Industrialization_in_Germany "Industrialization in Germany")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1c/Essen_a54_v_Stadtpanorama-1890.jpg/250px-Essen_a54_v_Stadtpanorama-1890.jpg)](/wiki/File:Essen_a54_v_Stadtpanorama-1890.jpg)

The [Krupp](/wiki/Krupp "Krupp") works in [Essen](/wiki/Essen "Essen"), 1890

For 30 years, Germany struggled against Britain to be Europe's leading industrial power. Representative of Germany's industry was the steel giant [Krupp](/wiki/Krupp "Krupp"), whose first factory was built in [Essen](/wiki/Essen "Essen"). By 1902, the factory alone became "A great city with its own streets, its own police force, fire department and traffic laws. There are 150 kilometers of rail, 60 different factory buildings, 8,500 machine tools, seven electrical stations, 140 kilometers of underground cable, and 46 overhead."[[47]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETaylor,_Edmund1967206-49)

Under Bismarck, Germany was a world innovator in building the [welfare state](/wiki/Welfare_state "Welfare state"). German workers enjoyed health, accident and maternity benefits, canteens, changing rooms, and a national pension scheme.[[48]](#cite_note-Hennock-50)

Industrialisation progressed dynamically in Germany, and German manufacturers began to capture domestic markets from British imports, and also to compete with British industry abroad, particularly in the U.S. The German textile and metal industries had by 1870 surpassed those of Britain in organisation and technical efficiency and superseded British manufacturers in the domestic market. Germany became the dominant economic power on the continent and was the second-largest exporting nation after Britain.[[49]](#cite_note-51)

Technological progress during German industrialisation occurred in four waves: the railway wave (1877–1886), the dye wave (1887–1896), the chemical wave (1897–1902), and the wave of electrical engineering (1903–1918).[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStreb2006347–373-52) Since Germany industrialised later than Britain, it was able to model its factories after those of Britain, thus making more efficient use of its capital and avoiding legacy methods in its leap to the envelope of technology. Germany invested more heavily than the British in research, especially in chemistry, ICE engines and electricity. Germany's dominance in physics and chemistry was such that one-third of all Nobel Prizes went to German inventors and researchers. The German [cartel](/wiki/Cartel "Cartel") system (known as *Konzerne*), being significantly concentrated, was able to make more efficient use of capital. Germany was not weighted down with an expensive worldwide empire that needed defense. Following Germany's annexation of [Alsace-Lorraine](/wiki/Alsace-Lorraine "Alsace-Lorraine") in 1871, it absorbed parts of what had been France's industrial base.[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBroadberryO'Rourke2010-53)

Germany overtook British steel production in 1893 and pig iron production in 1903. The German steel and pig iron production continued its rapid expansion: Between 1911 and 1913, the German steel and pig iron output reached one quarter of total global production.[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETorp201463-54)

German factories were larger and more modern than their British and French counterparts.[[53]](#cite_note-Germany_article_of_Encyclopedia_Britannia-55) By 1913, the German electricity production was higher than the combined electricity production of Britain, France, Italy and Sweden.[[54]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBerend201628-56)

By 1900, the German chemical industry dominated the world market for [synthetic dyes](/wiki/Dye "Dye").[[55]](#cite_note-57) The three major firms [BASF](/wiki/BASF "BASF"),[[56]](#cite_note-58) [Bayer](/wiki/Bayer "Bayer") and [Hoechst](/wiki/Hoechst_AG "Hoechst AG") produced several hundred different dyes, along with the five smaller firms. Imperial Germany built up the world's largest chemical industry, the production of German chemical industry was 60% higher than that of the United States.[[54]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBerend201628-56) In 1913, these eight firms produced almost 90% of the world supply of dyestuffs and sold about 80% of their production abroad. The three major firms had also integrated upstream into the production of essential raw materials, and they began to expand into other areas of chemistry such as [pharmaceuticals](/wiki/Drug "Drug"), [photographic film](/wiki/Photographic_film "Photographic film"), [agricultural chemicals](/wiki/Agrichemical "Agrichemical") and [electrochemicals](/wiki/Electrochemistry "Electrochemistry"). Top-level decision-making was in the hands of professional salaried managers; leading Chandler to call the German dye companies "the world's first truly managerial industrial enterprises".[[57]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChandler2004474–475-59) There were many spinoffs from research—such as the pharmaceutical industry, which emerged from chemical research.[[58]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurhop2009475–477-60)

By the start of [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") (1914–1918), German industry switched to war production. The heaviest demands were for coal and steel for artillery and shell production, and for chemicals for the synthesis of materials that were subject to import restrictions and for chemical weapons and war supplies.

##### Railways

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=German_Empire&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Railways")]

Lacking a technological base at first, the Germans imported their engineering and hardware from Britain but quickly learned the skills needed to operate and expand the railways. In many cities, the new railway shops were the centers of technological awareness and training, so that by 1850, Germany was self-sufficient in meeting the demands of railroad construction, and the railways were a major impetus for the growth of the new steel industry. German unification in 1870 stimulated consolidation, nationalisation into state-owned companies, and further rapid growth. Unlike the situation in France, the goal was support of industrialisation, and so heavy lines crisscrossed the [Ruhr](/wiki/Ruhr "Ruhr") and other industrial districts and provided good connections to the major ports of [Hamburg](/wiki/Hamburg "Hamburg") and [Bremen](/wiki/Bremen "Bremen"). By 1880, Germany had 9,400 locomotives, pulling 43,000 passengers and 30,000 tons of freight per year per locomotive,[[59]](#cite_note-61) and its rail network forged ahead of France.[[60]](#cite_note-62) The total length of German railroad tracks expanded from 21,000 km (13,000 mi) in 1871 to 63,000 km (39,000 mi) by 1913, establishing the second largest rail network in the world after the United States.[[61]](#cite_note-63) The German rail network was followed by Austria-Hungary (43,280 km; 26,890 mi), France (40,770 km; 25,330 mi), the United Kingdom (32,623 km; 20,271 mi), Italy (18,873 km; 11,727 mi) and Spain (15,088 km; 9,375 mi).[[62]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBroadberryO'Rourke2010[httpsbooksgooglecombooksidYHk0z-ujS3ACpgPA80_80]-64)

#### Consolidation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=German_Empire&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Consolidation")]

The creation of the Empire under Prussian leadership was a victory for the concept of *[Kleindeutschland](/wiki/Kleindeutschland_and_Gro%C3%9Fdeutschland "Kleindeutschland and Großdeutschland")* (Smaller Germany) over the *Großdeutschland* concept. This meant that Austria-Hungary, a multi-ethnic Empire with a considerable German-speaking population, would remain outside of the German nation state. Bismarck's policy was to pursue a solution diplomatically.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The effective alliance between Germany and Austria played a major role in Germany's decision to enter [World War I](/wiki/Causes_of_World_War_I "Causes of World War I") in 1914.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Bismarck announced there would be no more territorial additions to Germany in Europe, and his diplomacy after 1871 was focused on stabilizing the European system and preventing any wars. He succeeded, and only after his departure from office in 1890 did the diplomatic tensions start rising again.[[63]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGrenville2000342-65)

#### Social issues

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=German_Empire&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Social issues")]

After achieving formal unification in 1871, Bismarck devoted much of his attention to the cause of national unity. He opposed Catholic civil rights and emancipation, especially the influence of the [Vatican](/wiki/Holy_See "Holy See") under [Pope Pius IX](/wiki/Pope_Pius_IX "Pope Pius IX"), and working-class radicalism, represented by the emerging [Social Democratic Party](/wiki/Social_Democratic_Party_of_Germany "Social Democratic Party of Germany").

##### Kulturkampf

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=German_Empire&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Kulturkampf")]

Main article: [Kulturkampf](/wiki/Kulturkampf "Kulturkampf")

See also: [Pope Pius IX and Germany](/wiki/Pope_Pius_IX_and_Germany "Pope Pius IX and Germany")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/81/Kladderadatsch_1875_-_Zwischen_Berlin_und_Rom.png/500px-Kladderadatsch_1875_-_Zwischen_Berlin_und_Rom.png)](/wiki/File:Kladderadatsch_1875_-_Zwischen_Berlin_und_Rom.png)

Tensions between Germany and the Catholic Church hierarchy, depicted in a chess game between Bismarck and [Pope Pius IX](/wiki/Pope_Pius_IX "Pope Pius IX"). *Between Berlin and Rome*, [Kladderadatsch](/wiki/Kladderadatsch "Kladderadatsch"), 1875.

Prussia in 1871 included 16,000,000 Protestants, both Reformed and Lutheran, and 8,000,000 Catholics. Most people were generally segregated into their own religious worlds, living in rural districts or city neighbourhoods that were overwhelmingly of the same religion, and sending their children to separate public schools where their religion was taught. There was little interaction or intermarriage. On the whole, the Protestants had a higher social status, and the Catholics were more likely to be peasant farmers or unskilled or semiskilled industrial workers. In 1870, the Catholics formed their own political party, the [Centre Party](/wiki/Centre_Party_(Germany) "Centre Party (Germany)"), which generally supported unification and most of Bismarck's policies. However, Bismarck distrusted parliamentary democracy in general and opposition parties in particular, especially when the Centre Party showed signs of gaining support among dissident elements such as the Polish Catholics in [Silesia](/wiki/Silesia "Silesia"). A powerful intellectual force of the time was [anti-Catholicism](/wiki/Anti-Catholicism "Anti-Catholicism"), led by the liberal intellectuals who formed a vital part of Bismarck's coalition. They saw the Catholic Church as a powerful force of reaction and anti-modernity, especially after the proclamation of [papal infallibility](/wiki/Papal_infallibility "Papal infallibility") in 1870, and the tightening control of the Vatican over the local bishops.[[64]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELamberti2001169–187-66)

The Kulturkampf launched by Bismarck 1871–1880 affected Prussia; although there were similar movements in Baden and Hesse, the rest of Germany was not affected. According to the new imperial constitution, the states were in charge of religious and educational affairs; they funded the Protestant and Catholic schools. In July 1871 Bismarck abolished the Catholic section of the Prussian Ministry of ecclesiastical and educational affairs, depriving Catholics of their voice at the highest level. The system of strict [government supervision of schools](/wiki/State_education "State education") was applied only in Catholic areas; the Protestant schools were left alone.[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELamberti2001177-67)

Much more serious were the May laws of 1873. One made the appointment of any priest dependent on his attendance at a German university, as opposed to the seminaries that the Catholics typically used. Furthermore, all candidates for the ministry had to pass an examination in German culture before a state board which weeded out intransigent Catholics. Another provision gave the government veto power over most church activities. A second law abolished the jurisdiction of the Vatican over the Catholic Church in Prussia; its authority was transferred to a government body controlled by Protestants.[[66]](#cite_note-68)

Nearly all German bishops, clergy, and laymen rejected the legality of the new laws, and were defiant in the face of heavier and heavier penalties and imprisonments imposed by Bismarck's government. By 1876, all the Prussian bishops were imprisoned or in exile, and a third of the Catholic parishes were without a priest. In the face of systematic defiance, the Bismarck government increased both the penalties and its attacks. They were challenged in 1875 when a papal encyclical declared the whole ecclesiastical legislation of Prussia invalid and threatened to excommunicate any Catholic who obeyed. There was no violence, but the Catholics mobilized their support, set up numerous civic organizations, raised money to pay fines, and rallied behind their church and the Centre Party. The "Old Catholic Church", which rejected the [First Vatican Council](/wiki/First_Vatican_Council "First Vatican Council"), attracted only a few thousand members. Bismarck, a devout pietistic Protestant, realized his Kulturkampf was backfiring when secular and socialist elements used the opportunity to attack all religion. In the long run, the most significant result was the mobilization of Catholic voters and their insistence on protecting their religious identity. In the elections of 1874, the Centre Party doubled its popular vote and became the second-largest party in the national parliament—and remained a powerful force for the next 60 years, so that after Bismarck it became difficult to form a government without their support.[[67]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHolborn1969258–260-69)[[68]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEClark2006568–576-70)

##### Social reform

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=German_Empire&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Social reform")]

Bismarck built on a tradition of welfare programs in Prussia and Saxony that began as early as the 1840s. In the 1880s he introduced old-age pensions, accident insurance, medical care and unemployment insurance that formed the basis of the modern [European welfare state](/wiki/European_welfare_state "European welfare state"). He came to realize that this sort of policy was very appealing, since it bound workers to the state, and also fit in very well with his authoritarian nature. The social security systems installed by Bismarck (health care in 1883, accident insurance in 1884, invalidity and old-age insurance in 1889) at the time were the largest in the world and, to a degree, still exist in Germany today.

Bismarck's paternalistic programs won the support of German industry because its goals were to win the support of the working classes for the Empire and reduce the outflow of immigrants to America, where wages were higher but welfare did not exist.[[48]](#cite_note-Hennock-50)[[69]](#cite_note-71) Bismarck further won the support of both industry and skilled workers by his high tariff policies, which protected profits and wages from American competition, although they alienated the liberal intellectuals who wanted free trade.[[70]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpencer197940–64-72)[[71]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELambi196259–70-73)

##### Antisemitism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=German_Empire&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Antisemitism")]

As it was throughout Europe at the time, [antisemitism](/wiki/Antisemitism "Antisemitism") was endemic in Germany during the period. Before [Napoleon](/wiki/Napoleon "Napoleon")'s decrees ended the ghettos in the [Confederation of the Rhine](/wiki/Confederation_of_the_Rhine "Confederation of the Rhine"), it had been religiously motivated, but by the 19th century, it was a factor in [German nationalism](/wiki/German_nationalism "German nationalism"). In the popular mind, Jews became a symbol of capitalism and wealth. On the other hand, the constitution and legal system protected the rights of Jews as German citizens. Antisemitic parties were formed but soon collapsed.[[72]](#cite_note-74) But after the [Treaty of Versailles](/wiki/Treaty_of_Versailles "Treaty of Versailles"), and [Adolf Hitler](/wiki/Adolf_Hitler "Adolf Hitler")'s rise to power in [Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany"), antisemitism in Germany would increase.[[73]](#cite_note-75)

##### Germanisation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=German_Empire&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Germanisation")]

Main article: [Germanisation](/wiki/Germanisation "Germanisation")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bf/Rugi_pruskie.jpg/330px-Rugi_pruskie.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rugi_pruskie.jpg)

[Prussian deportations](/wiki/Prussian_deportations "Prussian deportations") of ethnic [Poles](/wiki/Polish_people "Polish people") *(Polenausweisungen)*, 1909 painting by [Wojciech Kossak](/wiki/Wojciech_Kossak "Wojciech Kossak")

One of the effects of the unification policies was the gradually increasing tendency to eliminate the use of non-German languages in public life, schools and academic settings with the intent of pressuring the non-German population to abandon their national identity in what was called "[Germanisation](/wiki/Germanisation "Germanisation")". These policies often had the reverse effect of stimulating resistance, usually in the form of homeschooling and tighter unity in the minority groups, especially the [Poles](/wiki/Polish_people "Polish people").[[74]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBaycroftHewitson2006166-76)

The Germanisation policies were targeted particularly [against the significant Polish minority](/wiki/Germanisation_of_Poles_during_the_Partitions "Germanisation of Poles during the Partitions") of the empire, gained by Prussia in the [partitions of Poland](/wiki/Partitions_of_Poland "Partitions of Poland"). Poles were treated as an [ethnic minority](/wiki/Ethnic_minority "Ethnic minority") even where they made up the majority, as in the [Province of Posen](/wiki/Province_of_Posen "Province of Posen"), where a series of anti-Polish measures was enforced.[[75]](#cite_note-77) Numerous anti-Polish laws had no great effect especially in the province of Posen where the German-speaking population dropped from 42.8% in 1871 to 38.1% in 1905, despite all efforts.[[76]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBroszat1978144-78)

#### Law

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=German_Empire&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Law")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f6/Meyers_b18_s0533a.jpg/250px-Meyers_b18_s0533a.jpg)](/wiki/File:Meyers_b18_s0533a.jpg)

Crime; convicts in relation to the population, 1882–1886

Bismarck's efforts also initiated the levelling of the enormous differences between the German states, which had been independent in their evolution for centuries, especially with legislation. The completely different legal histories and judicial systems posed enormous complications, especially for national trade. While a common trade code had already been introduced by the [Confederation](/wiki/German_Confederation "German Confederation") in 1861 (which was adapted for the Empire and, with great modifications, is still in effect today), there was little similarity in laws otherwise.

In 1871, a common [criminal code](/w/index.php?title=Reich_criminal_code&action=edit&redlink=1 "Reich criminal code (page does not exist)") [[de](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Reichsstrafgesetzbuch "de:Reichsstrafgesetzbuch")] was introduced; in 1877, common court procedures were established in the court system by the [courts constitution act](/w/index.php?title=Courts_constitution_act&action=edit&redlink=1 "Courts constitution act (page does not exist)") [[de](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gerichtsverfassungsgesetz "de:Gerichtsverfassungsgesetz")], code of civil procedure (*[Zivilprozessordnung](/wiki/Zivilprozessordnung_(Germany) "Zivilprozessordnung (Germany)")*) and code of criminal procedure (*[Strafprozessordnung](/wiki/Strafprozessordnung "Strafprozessordnung")[[de](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Strafprozessordnung_(Deutschland) "de:Strafprozessordnung (Deutschland)")]*). In 1873 the constitution was amended to allow the Empire to replace the various and greatly differing Civil Codes of the states (If they existed at all; for example, parts of Germany formerly occupied by Napoleon's France had adopted the French Civil Code, while in Prussia the *[Allgemeines Preußisches Landrecht](/wiki/General_State_Laws_for_the_Prussian_States "General State Laws for the Prussian States")* of 1794 was still in effect). In 1881, a first commission was established to produce a common Civil Code for all of the Empire, an enormous effort that would produce the *[Bürgerliches Gesetzbuch](/wiki/B%C3%BCrgerliches_Gesetzbuch "Bürgerliches Gesetzbuch")* (BGB), possibly one of the most impressive legal works in the world; it was eventually put into effect on 1 January 1900. All of these [codifications](/wiki/Codification_(law) "Codification (law)") are, albeit with many amendments, still in effect today.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

* [![Different legal systems in Germany prior to 1900](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b0/Legal_systems_in_1895_Germany.png/250px-Legal_systems_in_1895_Germany.png)](/wiki/File:Legal_systems_in_1895_Germany.png "Different legal systems in Germany prior to 1900")

  Different legal systems in Germany prior to 1900
* [![Fields of law in the German Empire](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/18/DR_Fields_of_Law.png/250px-DR_Fields_of_Law.png)](/wiki/File:DR_Fields_of_Law.png "Fields of law in the German Empire")

  Fields of law in the German Empire

### Year of the three emperors

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=German_Empire&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Year of the three emperors")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d8/Emperor_Friedrich_III.png/250px-Emperor_Friedrich_III.png)](/wiki/File:Emperor_Friedrich_III.png)

[Frederick III](/wiki/Frederick_III,_German_Emperor "Frederick III, German Emperor") was emperor for only 99 days (9 March – 15 June 1888)

Main article: [Year of the Three Emperors](/wiki/Year_of_the_Three_Emperors "Year of the Three Emperors")

On 9 March 1888, Wilhelm I died shortly before his 91st birthday, leaving his son [Frederick](/wiki/Frederick_III,_German_Emperor "Frederick III, German Emperor") as the new emperor. Frederick was a liberal and an admirer of the British constitution,[[77]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKitchen2000214-79) while his links to Britain strengthened further with his marriage to [Princess Victoria](/wiki/Victoria,_Princess_Royal "Victoria, Princess Royal"), eldest child of [Queen Victoria](/wiki/Queen_Victoria "Queen Victoria"). With his ascent to the throne, many hoped that Frederick's reign would lead to a [liberalization](/wiki/Liberalization "Liberalization") of the Reich and an increase of parliament's influence on the political process. The dismissal of [Robert von Puttkamer](/wiki/Robert_von_Puttkamer "Robert von Puttkamer"), the highly conservative [Prussian interior minister](/wiki/Interior_Minister_of_Prussia "Interior Minister of Prussia"), on 8 June was a sign of the expected direction and a blow to Bismarck's administration.

By the time of his accession, however, Frederick had developed incurable [laryngeal cancer](/wiki/Laryngeal_cancer "Laryngeal cancer"), which had been diagnosed in 1887. He died on the 99th day of his rule, on 15 June 1888. His son [Wilhelm](/wiki/Wilhelm_II "Wilhelm II") became emperor.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Wilhelmine era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=German_Empire&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Wilhelmine era")]

Main article: [Wilhelminism](/wiki/Wilhelminism "Wilhelminism")

#### Bismarck's resignation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=German_Empire&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Bismarck's resignation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6e/Kaiser_Wilhelm_II_of_Germany_-_1902.jpg/250px-Kaiser_Wilhelm_II_of_Germany_-_1902.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kaiser_Wilhelm_II_of_Germany_-_1902.jpg)

[Wilhelm II](/wiki/Wilhelm_II "Wilhelm II") in 1902

[Wilhelm II](/wiki/Wilhelm_II "Wilhelm II") wanted to reassert his ruling prerogatives at a time when other monarchs in Europe were being transformed into constitutional figureheads. This decision led the ambitious Kaiser into conflict with Bismarck. The old chancellor had hoped to guide Wilhelm as he had guided his grandfather, but the emperor wanted to be the master in his own house and had many sycophants telling him that Frederick the Great would not have been great with a Bismarck at his side.[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKurtz197060-80) A key difference between Wilhelm II and Bismarck was their approaches to handling political crises, especially in 1889, when German coal miners went on strike in [Upper Silesia](/wiki/Upper_Silesia "Upper Silesia"). Bismarck demanded that the [German Army](/wiki/German_Army_(German_Empire) "German Army (German Empire)") be sent in to crush the strike, but Wilhelm II rejected this authoritarian measure, responding "I do not wish to stain my reign with the blood of my subjects."[[79]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStürmer200063-81) Instead of condoning repression, Wilhelm had the government negotiate with a delegation from the coal miners, which brought the strike to an end without violence. The fractious relationship ended in March 1890, after Wilhelm II and Bismarck quarrelled, and the chancellor resigned days later.[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKurtz197060-80)

With Bismarck's departure, Wilhelm II became the dominant ruler of Germany. Unlike his grandfather, Wilhelm I, who had been largely content to leave government affairs to the chancellor, Wilhelm II wanted to be fully informed and actively involved in running Germany, not an ornamental figurehead, although most Germans found his claims of divine right to rule amusing. Wilhelm allowed politician [Walther Rathenau](/wiki/Walther_Rathenau "Walther Rathenau") to tutor him in European economics and industrial and financial realities in Europe.[[80]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKurtz197063-82)

As [Isabel V. Hull](/wiki/Isabel_V._Hull "Isabel V. Hull") noted in 2024, Bismarckian foreign policy "was too sedate for the reckless Kaiser".[[81]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHull200485-83) Wilhelm became internationally notorious for his aggressive stance on foreign policy and his strategic blunders (such as the [Tangier Crisis](/wiki/First_Moroccan_Crisis "First Moroccan Crisis")), which pushed the German Empire into growing political isolation and eventually helped to cause [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I").

#### Domestic affairs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=German_Empire&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Domestic affairs")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ca/Reichstagsgebaeude.jpg/250px-Reichstagsgebaeude.jpg)](/wiki/File:Reichstagsgebaeude.jpg)

The *[Reichstag](/wiki/Reichstag_building "Reichstag building")* in the 1890s / early 1900s

Under Wilhelm II, Germany no longer had long-ruling strong chancellors like Bismarck. The new chancellors had difficulty in performing their roles, especially the additional role as [Prime Minister of Prussia](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Prussia "Prime Minister of Prussia") assigned to them in the German Constitution. The reforms of Chancellor [Leo von Caprivi](/wiki/Leo_von_Caprivi "Leo von Caprivi"), which liberalized trade and so reduced unemployment, were supported by the Kaiser and most Germans except for Prussian landowners, who feared loss of land and power and launched several campaigns against the reforms.[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKurtz197067-84)

While Prussian aristocrats challenged the demands of a united German state, in the 1890s several organizations were set up to challenge the authoritarian conservative Prussian militarism which was being imposed on the country. Educators opposed to the German state-run schools, which emphasized military education, set up their own independent liberal schools, which encouraged individuality and freedom.[[83]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKurtz197072-85) However nearly all the schools in Imperial Germany had a very high standard and kept abreast with modern developments in knowledge.[[84]](#cite_note-86)

Artists began experimental art in opposition to Kaiser Wilhelm's support for traditional art, to which Wilhelm responded "art which transgresses the laws and limits laid down by me can no longer be called art".[[85]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKurtz197076-87) It was largely thanks to Wilhelm's influence that most printed material in Germany used [blackletter](/wiki/Blackletter "Blackletter") instead of the Roman type used in the rest of Western Europe. At the same time, a new generation of cultural creators emerged.[[86]](#cite_note-88)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c5/Berlin_Unter_den_Linden_Cafe_Bauer_um_1900.jpg/250px-Berlin_Unter_den_Linden_Cafe_Bauer_um_1900.jpg)](/wiki/File:Berlin_Unter_den_Linden_Cafe_Bauer_um_1900.jpg)

Berlin in the late 19th century

From the 1890s onwards, the most effective opposition to the monarchy came from the newly formed [Social Democratic Party of Germany](/wiki/Social_Democratic_Party_of_Germany "Social Democratic Party of Germany") (SPD), whose radicals advocated [Marxism](/wiki/Marxism "Marxism"). The threat of the SPD to the German monarchy and industrialists caused the state both to crack down on the party's supporters and to implement its own programme of social reform to soothe discontent. Germany's large industries provided significant social welfare programmes and good care to their employees, as long as they were not identified as socialists or trade-union members. The larger industrial firms provided pensions, sickness benefits and even housing to their employees.[[83]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKurtz197072-85)

Having learned from the failure of Bismarck's [Kulturkampf](/wiki/Kulturkampf "Kulturkampf"), Wilhelm II maintained good relations with the Roman Catholic Church and concentrated on opposing socialism.[[87]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKurtz197056-89) This policy failed when the Social Democrats won a third of the votes in the [1912 elections](/wiki/1912_German_federal_election "1912 German federal election") to the *Reichstag* and became the largest political party in Germany. The government remained in the hands of a succession of conservative coalitions supported by right-wing liberals or Catholic clerics and heavily dependent on the Kaiser's favour. The rising militarism under Wilhelm II caused many Germans to emigrate to the U.S. and the British colonies to escape mandatory military service.

During World War I, the Kaiser increasingly devolved his powers to the leaders of the German High Command, particularly future [German president](/wiki/President_of_Germany "President of Germany"), Field Marshal [Paul von Hindenburg](/wiki/Paul_von_Hindenburg "Paul von Hindenburg") and *Generalquartiermeister* [Erich Ludendorff](/wiki/Erich_Ludendorff "Erich Ludendorff"). Hindenburg took over the role of commander–in–chief from the Kaiser, while Ludendorff became de facto general chief of staff. By 1916, Germany was effectively a military dictatorship run by Hindenburg and Ludendorff, with the Kaiser reduced to a mere figurehead.[[88]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECecil1996ch._9–13-90)

#### Foreign affairs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=German_Empire&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Foreign affairs")]

Further information: [International relations (1814–1919)](/wiki/International_relations_(1814%E2%80%931919) "International relations (1814–1919)")

##### Colonialism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=German_Empire&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Colonialism")]

Main article: [German colonial empire](/wiki/German_colonial_empire "German colonial empire")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6b/Kongokonferenz.jpg/250px-Kongokonferenz.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kongokonferenz.jpg)

[Bismarck](/wiki/Otto_von_Bismarck "Otto von Bismarck") at the [Berlin Conference](/wiki/Berlin_Conference "Berlin Conference"), 1884

Wilhelm II wanted Germany to have her "[place in the sun](/wiki/Place_in_the_sun "Place in the sun")", like Britain, which he constantly wished to emulate or rival.[[89]](#cite_note-91) With German traders and merchants already active worldwide, he encouraged colonial efforts in Africa and the Pacific ("[new imperialism](/wiki/New_imperialism "New imperialism")"), causing the German Empire to vie with other European powers for remaining "unclaimed" territories. With the encouragement or at least the acquiescence of Britain, which at this stage saw Germany as a counterweight to her old rival France, Germany acquired [German Southwest Africa](/wiki/German_South_West_Africa "German South West Africa") (modern [Namibia](/wiki/Namibia "Namibia")), [German Kamerun](/wiki/Kamerun "Kamerun") (modern [Cameroon](/wiki/Cameroon "Cameroon")), [Togoland](/wiki/Togoland "Togoland") (modern [Togo](/wiki/Togo "Togo")) and [German East Africa](/wiki/German_East_Africa "German East Africa") (modern [Rwanda](/wiki/Rwanda "Rwanda"), [Burundi](/wiki/Burundi "Burundi"), and the mainland part of current [Tanzania](/wiki/Tanzania "Tanzania")). Islands were gained in the Pacific through purchase and treaties and also a 99-year lease for the territory of [Jiaozhou](/wiki/Jiaozhou_Bay_Leased_Territory "Jiaozhou Bay Leased Territory") in northeast China. But of these German colonies only Togoland and [German Samoa](/wiki/German_Samoa "German Samoa") (after 1908) became self-sufficient and profitable; all the others required subsidies from the Berlin treasury for building infrastructure, school systems, hospitals and other institutions.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cd/Reichskolonialflagge.svg/250px-Reichskolonialflagge.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Reichskolonialflagge.svg)

Flag of the German colonial empire[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Bismarck had originally dismissed the agitation for colonies with contempt; he favoured a Eurocentric foreign policy, as the treaty arrangements made during his tenure in office show. As a latecomer to colonization, Germany repeatedly came into conflict with the established colonial powers and also with the United States, which opposed German attempts at colonial expansion in both the Caribbean and the Pacific. Native insurrections in German territories received prominent coverage in other countries, especially in Britain; the established powers had dealt with such uprisings decades earlier, often brutally, and had secured firm control of their colonies by then. The [Boxer Rising](/wiki/Boxer_Rebellion "Boxer Rebellion") in China, which the Chinese government eventually sponsored, began in the Shandong province, in part because Germany, as colonizer at [Jiaozhou](/wiki/Jiaozhou_Bay_Leased_Territory "Jiaozhou Bay Leased Territory"), was an untested power and had only been active there for two years. Seven western nations, including the United States, and Japan mounted a joint relief force to rescue westerners caught up in the rebellion. During the departure ceremonies for the German contingent, Wilhelm II urged them to behave like the [Hun](/wiki/Hun "Hun") invaders of continental Europe – an unfortunate remark that would later be resurrected by British propagandists to paint Germans as barbarians during [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") and [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II")[*[according to whom?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Words_to_watch#Unsupported_attributions "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Words to watch")*]. On two occasions, a French-German conflict over the fate of Morocco seemed inevitable.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e2/Herero_chained.jpg/250px-Herero_chained.jpg)](/wiki/File:Herero_chained.jpg)

Prisoners from the [Herero](/wiki/Herero_people "Herero people") tribe during the 1904-1908 revolt

Upon acquiring Southwest Africa, German settlers were encouraged to cultivate land held by the [Herero](/wiki/Herero_people "Herero people") and [Nama](/wiki/Nama_people "Nama people"). Herero and Nama tribal lands were used for a variety of exploitative goals (much as the British did before in [Rhodesia](/wiki/Rhodesia "Rhodesia")), including farming, ranching, and mining for minerals and [diamonds](/wiki/Diamonds "Diamonds"). In 1904, the Herero and the Nama revolted against the colonists in Southwest Africa, killing farm families, their laborers and servants. In response to the attacks, troops were dispatched to quell the uprising which then resulted in the [Herero and Nama genocide](/wiki/Herero_and_Nama_genocide "Herero and Nama genocide"). In total, some 65,000 Herero (80% of the total Herero population), and 10,000 Nama (50% of the total Nama population) perished. The commander of the punitive expedition, General [Lothar von Trotha](/wiki/Lothar_von_Trotha "Lothar von Trotha"), was eventually relieved and reprimanded for his usurpation of orders and the cruelties he inflicted. These occurrences were sometimes referred to as "the first genocide of the 20th century" and officially condemned by the United Nations in 1985. In 2004 a formal apology by a government minister of the Federal Republic of Germany followed.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

##### Middle East

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=German_Empire&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Middle East")]

Bismarck and [Wilhelm II](/wiki/Wilhelm_II "Wilhelm II") after him sought closer economic ties with the [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire"). Under Wilhelm II, with the financial backing of the [Deutsche Bank](/wiki/Deutsche_Bank "Deutsche Bank"), the [Baghdad Railway](/wiki/Baghdad_Railway "Baghdad Railway") was begun in 1900, although by 1914 it was still 500 km (310 mi) short of its destination in Baghdad.[[90]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStürmer200091-92) In an interview with Wilhelm in 1899, [Cecil Rhodes](/wiki/Cecil_Rhodes "Cecil Rhodes") had tried "to convince the Kaiser that the future of the German empire abroad lay in the Middle East" and not in Africa; with a grand Middle-Eastern empire, Germany could afford to allow Britain the unhindered completion of the Cape-to-Cairo railway that Rhodes favoured.[[91]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELouis1963163-93) Britain initially supported the [Baghdad Railway](/wiki/Baghdad_Railway "Baghdad Railway"); but by 1911 British statesmen came to fear it might be extended to [Basra](/wiki/Basra "Basra") on the [Persian Gulf](/wiki/Persian_Gulf "Persian Gulf"), threatening Britain's naval supremacy in the Indian Ocean. Accordingly, they asked to have construction halted, to which Germany and the Ottoman Empire acquiesced.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

##### South America

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=German_Empire&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: South America")]

In South America, Germany's primary interest was in [Argentina, Brazil, Chile](/wiki/ABC_countries "ABC countries"), and [Uruguay](/wiki/Uruguay "Uruguay") and viewed the countries of northern South America—[Ecuador](/wiki/Ecuador "Ecuador"), [Colombia](/wiki/Colombia "Colombia"), and [Venezuela](/wiki/Venezuela "Venezuela")—as a buffer to protect its interest from the growing influence of the United States.[[92]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFischer2008135–160-94) Policymakers in Germany analysed the possibility of establishing bases in [Margarita Island](/wiki/Margarita_Island "Margarita Island") and showed interest in the [Galápagos Islands](/wiki/Gal%C3%A1pagos_Islands "Galápagos Islands") but soon abandoned any such designs given that far-flung bases in northern South America would be very vulnerable.[[93]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMitchell199967-95)[[92]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFischer2008135–160-94) Germany attempted to promote Chile, a country that was [heavily influenced by Germany](/wiki/German_influence_in_Chile "German influence in Chile"),[[94]](#cite_note-Carlos2011-96) into a regional counterweight to the United States.[[95]](#cite_note-Fischer2008-97) Germany and Britain managed through Chile to have Ecuador deny the United States a naval base in the [Galápagos Islands](/wiki/Gal%C3%A1pagos_Islands "Galápagos Islands").[[95]](#cite_note-Fischer2008-97)

Claims that German communities in South America acted as extensions of the German Empire were ubiquitous by 1900 but it has never been proved that these communities acted in such way to any significant degree.[[96]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPenny2017519–549-98) German political, cultural and scientific influence was particularly intense in Chile in the decades before World War I, and the prestige of Germany and German things in Chile remained high after the war but did not recover to its pre-war levels.[[97]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESanhueza201129–40-99)[[96]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPenny2017519–549-98)

##### Pre-war Europe

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=German_Empire&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Pre-war Europe")]

Main articles: [Causes of World War I](/wiki/Causes_of_World_War_I "Causes of World War I") and [Diplomatic history of World War I](/wiki/Diplomatic_history_of_World_War_I "Diplomatic history of World War I")

Berlin was deeply suspicious of a supposed conspiracy of its enemies: that year-by-year in the early 20th century it was systematically encircled by enemies.[[98]](#cite_note-100) There was a growing fear that the supposed enemy coalition of Russia, France and Britain was getting stronger militarily every year, especially Russia. The longer Berlin waited the less likely it would prevail in a war.[[99]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESeligmann2002333–355-101) According to American historian [Gordon A. Craig](/wiki/Gordon_A._Craig "Gordon A. Craig"), it was after the set-back in Morocco in 1905 that the fear of encirclement began to be a potent factor in German politics."[[100]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECraig1978321-102) Few outside observers agreed with the notion of Germany as a victim of deliberate encirclement.[[101]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGeise1976121–138-103)[[102]](#cite_note-104) English historian [G. M. Trevelyan](/wiki/G._M._Trevelyan "G. M. Trevelyan") expressed the British viewpoint:

> The encirclement, such as it was, was of Germany's own making. She had encircled herself by alienating France over Alsace-Lorraine, Russia by her support of Austria-Hungary's anti-Slav policy in the Balkans, England by building her rival fleet. She had created with Austria-Hungary a military bloc in the heart of Europe so powerful and yet so restless that her neighbors on each side had no choice but either to become her vassals or to stand together for protection....They used their central position to create fear in all sides, in order to gain their diplomatic ends. And then they complained that on all sides they had been encircled.[[103]](#cite_note-105)

Wilhelm II, under pressure from his new advisors after Bismarck left, committed a fatal error when he decided to allow the "[Reinsurance Treaty](/wiki/Reinsurance_Treaty "Reinsurance Treaty")" that Bismarck had negotiated with Tsarist Russia to lapse. It allowed Russia to make a new alliance with France. Germany was left with no firm ally but [Austria-Hungary](/wiki/Austria-Hungary "Austria-Hungary"), and her support for action in annexing [Bosnia and Herzegovina](/wiki/Bosnia_and_Herzegovina "Bosnia and Herzegovina") in 1908 further soured relations with Russia. Berlin missed the opportunity to secure an alliance with Britain in the 1890s when it was involved in colonial rivalries with France, and it alienated British statesmen further by openly supporting the Boers in the [South African War](/wiki/South_African_War "South African War") and building a navy to rival Britain's. By 1911, Wilhelm had completely picked apart the careful power balance established by Bismarck and Britain turned to France in the [Entente Cordiale](/wiki/Entente_Cordiale "Entente Cordiale"). Germany's only other ally besides Austria was the [Kingdom of Italy](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Italy "Kingdom of Italy"), but it remained an ally only *pro forma*. When war came, Italy saw more benefit in an alliance with Britain, France, and Russia, which, in the secret [Treaty of London](/wiki/Treaty_of_London_(1915) "Treaty of London (1915)") in 1915 promised it the frontier districts of Austria and also colonial concessions. Germany did acquire a second ally in 1914 when the Ottoman Empire entered the war on its side, but in the long run, supporting the Ottoman war effort only drained away German resources from the main fronts.[[104]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECraig1978302–338,_350-106)

### World War I

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=German_Empire&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: World War I")]

See also: [History of Germany during World War I](/wiki/History_of_Germany_during_World_War_I "History of Germany during World War I")

#### Origins

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=German_Empire&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Origins")]

Main article: [German entry into World War I](/wiki/German_entry_into_World_War_I "German entry into World War I")
