[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4f/WWI-re.png/500px-WWI-re.png)](/wiki/File:WWI-re.png)

Map of the world showing the participants in World War I. Those fighting on the [Entente](/wiki/Triple_Entente "Triple Entente")'s side (at one point or another) are depicted in green, the [Central Powers](/wiki/Central_Powers "Central Powers") in orange, and neutral countries in grey.

Following the assassination of the Austro-Hungarian Archduke [Franz Ferdinand](/wiki/Archduke_Franz_Ferdinand_of_Austria "Archduke Franz Ferdinand of Austria") by [Gavrilo Princip](/wiki/Gavrilo_Princip "Gavrilo Princip"), the Kaiser offered Emperor [Franz Joseph](/wiki/Franz_Joseph_I_of_Austria "Franz Joseph I of Austria") full support for Austro-Hungarian plans to invade the [Kingdom of Serbia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Serbia "Kingdom of Serbia"), which Austria-Hungary blamed for the assassination. This unconditional support for Austria-Hungary was called a "blank cheque" by historians, including German [Fritz Fischer](/wiki/Fritz_Fischer_(historian) "Fritz Fischer (historian)"). Subsequent interpretation – for example at the [Versailles Peace Conference](/wiki/Versailles_Peace_Conference "Versailles Peace Conference") – was that this "blank cheque" licensed Austro-Hungarian aggression regardless of the diplomatic consequences, and thus [Germany bore responsibility for starting the war](/wiki/War_Guilt_Clause "War Guilt Clause"), or at least provoking a wider conflict.

Germany began the war by targeting its chief rival, France. Germany saw the French Republic as its principal danger on the European continent as it could mobilize much faster than Russia and bordered Germany's industrial core in the [Rhineland](/wiki/Rhineland "Rhineland"). Unlike Britain and Russia, the French entered the war mainly for revenge against Germany, in particular for France's [loss](/wiki/Franco-Prussian_War "Franco-Prussian War") of [Alsace-Lorraine](/wiki/Alsace-Lorraine "Alsace-Lorraine") to Germany in 1871. The German high command knew that France would muster its forces to go into Alsace-Lorraine. Aside from the very unofficial [Septemberprogramm](/wiki/Septemberprogramm "Septemberprogramm"), the Germans never stated a clear list of goals that they wanted out of the war.[[105]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStibbe2006176–178-107)

#### Western Front

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=German_Empire&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Western Front")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/63/Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-R19231%2C_Berlin%2C_Mobilmachung.jpg/250px-Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-R19231%2C_Berlin%2C_Mobilmachung.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-R19231,_Berlin,_Mobilmachung.jpg)

German troops being mobilized, 1914

Germany did not want to risk lengthy battles along the Franco-German border and instead adopted the [Schlieffen Plan](/wiki/Schlieffen_Plan "Schlieffen Plan"), a military strategy designed to cripple France by [invading Belgium](/wiki/Rape_of_Belgium "Rape of Belgium") and [Luxembourg](/wiki/Luxembourg "Luxembourg"), sweeping down to encircle and crush both Paris and the French forces along the Franco-German border in a quick victory. After defeating France, Germany would turn to attack Russia. The plan required violating the official neutrality of Belgium and Luxembourg, which [Britain](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire") had guaranteed by treaty. However, the Germans had calculated that Britain would enter the war regardless of whether they had formal justification to do so.[[106]](#cite_note-108) At first the attack was successful: the [German Army](/wiki/German_Army_(German_Empire) "German Army (German Empire)") swept down from Belgium and Luxembourg and advanced on Paris, at the nearby river [Marne](/wiki/Marne_(river) "Marne (river)"). However, the evolution of weapons over the last century heavily favored defense over offense, especially thanks to the machine gun, so that it took proportionally more offensive force to overcome a defensive position. This resulted in the German lines on the offense contracting to keep up the offensive timetable while correspondingly the French lines were extending. In addition, some German units that were originally slotted for the German far-right were transferred to the Eastern Front in reaction to Russia mobilizing far faster than anticipated. The combined effect had the German right flank sweeping down in front of Paris instead of behind it, exposing the German Right flank to the extending French lines and attack from strategic French reserves stationed in Paris. Attacking the exposed German right flank, the [French Army](/wiki/French_Army "French Army") and the [British Army](/wiki/British_Army "British Army") put up a strong resistance to the defense of Paris at the [First Battle of the Marne](/wiki/First_Battle_of_the_Marne "First Battle of the Marne"), resulting in the German Army retreating to defensive positions along the river [Aisne](/wiki/Aisne_(river) "Aisne (river)"). A subsequent [Race to the Sea](/wiki/Race_to_the_Sea "Race to the Sea") resulted in a long-held stalemate between the German Army and the Allies in dug-in [trench warfare](/wiki/Trench_warfare "Trench warfare") positions from [Alsace](/wiki/Alsace "Alsace") to [Flanders](/wiki/Flanders "Flanders").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e9/Karte_Brigadestandorte_des_Deutschen_Heers_1914.png/250px-Karte_Brigadestandorte_des_Deutschen_Heers_1914.png)](/wiki/File:Karte_Brigadestandorte_des_Deutschen_Heers_1914.png)

German Army positions, 1914

[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

German attempts to break through failed at the two battles of [Ypres](/wiki/Ypres "Ypres") ([1st](/wiki/First_Battle_of_Ypres "First Battle of Ypres")/[2nd](/wiki/Second_Battle_of_Ypres "Second Battle of Ypres")) with huge casualties. A series of allied offensives in 1915 against German positions in [Artois](/wiki/Second_Battle_of_Artois "Second Battle of Artois") and [Champagne](/wiki/Second_Battle_of_Champagne "Second Battle of Champagne") resulted in huge allied casualties and little territorial change. German [Chief of Staff](/wiki/Chief_of_Staff "Chief of Staff") [Erich von Falkenhayn](/wiki/Erich_von_Falkenhayn "Erich von Falkenhayn") decided to exploit the defensive advantages that had shown themselves in the 1915 Allied offensives by attempting to goad France into attacking strong defensive positions near the ancient city of [Verdun](/wiki/Verdun "Verdun"). Verdun had been one of the last cities to hold out against the German Army in 1870, and Falkenhayn predicted that as a matter of national pride the French would do anything to ensure that it was not taken. He expected that he could take strong defensive positions in the hills overlooking Verdun on the east bank of the river Meuse to threaten the city and the French would launch desperate attacks against these positions. He predicted that French losses would be greater than those of the Germans and that continued French commitment of troops to Verdun would "bleed the French Army white." In February 1916, the [Battle of Verdun](/wiki/Battle_of_Verdun "Battle of Verdun") began, with the French positions under constant shelling and poison gas attack and taking large casualties under the assault of overwhelmingly large German forces. However, Falkenhayn's prediction of a greater ratio of French killed proved to be wrong as both sides took heavy casualties. Falkenhayn was replaced by [Erich Ludendorff](/wiki/Erich_Ludendorff "Erich Ludendorff"), and with no success in sight, the German Army pulled out of Verdun in December 1916 and the battle ended.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### Eastern Front

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=German_Empire&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Eastern Front")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/73/Map_Treaty_of_Brest-Litovsk-de.svg/330px-Map_Treaty_of_Brest-Litovsk-de.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Map_Treaty_of_Brest-Litovsk-de.svg)

The [Eastern Front](/wiki/Eastern_Front_(World_War_I) "Eastern Front (World War I)") at the time of the cease-fire and the [Treaty of Brest-Litovsk](/wiki/Treaty_of_Brest-Litovsk "Treaty of Brest-Litovsk")

While the [Western Front](/wiki/Western_Front_(World_War_I) "Western Front (World War I)") was a stalemate for the German Army, the [Eastern Front](/wiki/Eastern_Front_(World_War_I) "Eastern Front (World War I)") eventually proved to be a great success. Despite initial setbacks due to the unexpectedly rapid mobilisation of the Russian army, which resulted in a Russian invasion of East Prussia and Austrian [Galicia](/wiki/Galicia_(Eastern_Europe) "Galicia (Eastern Europe)"), the badly organised and supplied [Russian Army](/wiki/Imperial_Russian_Army "Imperial Russian Army") [faltered](/wiki/Battle_of_Tannenberg "Battle of Tannenberg") and the German and Austro-Hungarian armies thereafter steadily advanced eastward. The Germans benefited from political instability in Russia and its population's desire to end the war. In 1917 the German government allowed Russia's communist [Bolshevik](/wiki/Bolshevik "Bolshevik") leader [Vladimir Lenin](/wiki/Vladimir_Lenin "Vladimir Lenin") to travel through Germany from [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland "Switzerland") into Russia. Germany believed that if Lenin could create further political unrest, Russia would no longer be able to continue its war with Germany, allowing the German Army to focus on the Western Front.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In March 1917, the [Tsar](/wiki/Tsar "Tsar") was ousted from the Russian throne, and in November a [Bolshevik](/wiki/Bolshevik "Bolshevik") government came to power under the leadership of Lenin. Facing political opposition, he decided to end Russia's campaign against Germany, Austria-Hungary, the [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire") and [Bulgaria](/wiki/Bulgaria "Bulgaria") to redirect Bolshevik energy to eliminating internal dissent. In March 1918, by the [Treaty of Brest-Litovsk](/wiki/Treaty_of_Brest-Litovsk_(Russia%E2%80%93Central_Powers) "Treaty of Brest-Litovsk (Russia–Central Powers)"), the Bolshevik government gave Germany and the Ottoman Empire enormous territorial and economic concessions in exchange for an end to war on the Eastern Front. All of present-day [Estonia](/wiki/Estonia "Estonia"), [Latvia](/wiki/Latvia "Latvia") and [Lithuania](/wiki/Lithuania "Lithuania") was given over to the German occupation authority *[Ober Ost](/wiki/Ober_Ost "Ober Ost")*, along with [Belarus](/wiki/Belarus "Belarus") and [Ukraine](/wiki/Ukraine "Ukraine"). Thus Germany had at last achieved its long-wanted dominance of "Mitteleuropa" (Central Europe) and could now focus fully on defeating the Allies on the Western Front. In practice, however, the forces that were needed to garrison and secure the new territories were a drain on the German war effort.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### Colonies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=German_Empire&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Colonies")]

Germany quickly lost almost all its colonies. However, in [German East Africa](/wiki/German_East_Africa "German East Africa"), a guerrilla campaign was waged by the colonial army leader there, General [Paul Emil von Lettow-Vorbeck](/wiki/Paul_von_Lettow-Vorbeck "Paul von Lettow-Vorbeck"). Using Germans and native [Askaris](/wiki/Askari "Askari"), Lettow-Vorbeck launched multiple guerrilla raids against British forces in [Kenya](/wiki/Kenya "Kenya") and [Rhodesia](/wiki/Rhodesia "Rhodesia"). He also invaded [Portuguese Mozambique](/wiki/Portuguese_Mozambique "Portuguese Mozambique") to gain his forces supplies and to pick up more Askari recruits. His force was still active at war's end.[[107]](#cite_note-109)

#### 1918

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=German_Empire&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: 1918")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4d/German_Empire_in_1918.png/250px-German_Empire_in_1918.png)](/wiki/File:German_Empire_in_1918.png)

The German Empire during [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), shortly before its collapse:

Home Territory (1871–1918)

[Puppet states](/wiki/Puppet_states "Puppet states") (1917–1918)

Occupied territory (1914–1918)

The defeat of Russia in 1917 enabled Germany to transfer hundreds of thousands of troops from the Eastern to the Western Front, giving it a numerical advantage over the [Allies](/wiki/Allies_of_World_War_I "Allies of World War I"). By retraining the soldiers in new [infiltration tactics](/wiki/Infiltration_tactics "Infiltration tactics"), the Germans expected to unfreeze the battlefield and win a decisive victory before the army of the United States, which had now entered the war on the side of the Allies, arrived in strength.[[108]](#cite_note-110) In what was known as the "Kaiserschlacht", Germany converged their troops and delivered multiple blows that pushed back the allies. However, the repeated German offensives in the spring of 1918 all failed, as the Allies fell back and regrouped and the Germans lacked the [reserves](/wiki/Military_reserve "Military reserve") needed to consolidate their gains. Meanwhile, soldiers had become radicalised by the [Russian Revolution](/wiki/Russian_Revolution "Russian Revolution") and were less willing to continue fighting. The war effort sparked civil unrest in Germany, while the troops, who had been constantly in the field without relief, grew exhausted and lost all hope of victory. In the summer of 1918, the British Army was at its peak strength with as many as 4.5 million men on the western front and 4,000 tanks for the Hundred Days Offensive, the Americans arriving at the rate of 10,000 a day, Germany's allies facing collapse and the German Empire's manpower exhausted, it was only a matter of time before multiple Allied offensives destroyed the German army.[[109]](#cite_note-111)

The concept of "[total war](/wiki/Total_war "Total war")" meant that supplies had to be redirected towards the armed forces and, with German commerce being stopped by the Allied [naval blockade](/wiki/Naval_blockade "Naval blockade"), German civilians were forced to live in increasingly meagre conditions. First [food prices](/wiki/Food_prices "Food prices") were controlled, then rationing was introduced. During the war about 750,000 German civilians died from malnutrition.[[110]](#cite_note-112)

Towards the end of the war, conditions deteriorated rapidly on the home front, with severe food shortages reported in all urban areas. The causes included the transfer of many farmers and food workers into the military, combined with the overburdened railway system, shortages of coal, and the British blockade. The winter of 1916–1917 was known as the "turnip winter" because the people had to survive on a vegetable more commonly reserved for livestock as a substitute for potatoes and meat, which were increasingly scarce. Thousands of soup kitchens were opened to feed the hungry, who grumbled that the farmers were keeping the food for themselves. Even the army had to cut the soldiers' rations.[[111]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChickering2004141–142-113) The morale of both civilians and soldiers continued to sink. The population of Germany was already suffering from outbreaks of disease due to malnutrition due to Allied blockade preventing food imports. [Spanish flu](/wiki/Spanish_flu "Spanish flu") arrived in Germany with returning troops. Around 287,000 people died of Spanish flu in Germany between 1918 and 1920 with 50,000 deaths in Berlin alone.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

##### Revolt and demise

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=German_Empire&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Revolt and demise")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a6/Bundesarchiv_Bild_175-01448%2C_Berlin%2C_Reichskanzlei%2C_Philipp_Scheidemann.jpg/250px-Bundesarchiv_Bild_175-01448%2C_Berlin%2C_Reichskanzlei%2C_Philipp_Scheidemann.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_Bild_175-01448,_Berlin,_Reichskanzlei,_Philipp_Scheidemann.jpg)

The [SPD](/wiki/Social_Democratic_Party_of_Germany "Social Democratic Party of Germany") leader [Philipp Scheidemann](/wiki/Philipp_Scheidemann "Philipp Scheidemann") [proclaims the republic for Germany](/wiki/Proclamation_of_the_republic_in_Germany "Proclamation of the republic in Germany") from the [Reichstag building](/wiki/Reichstag_building "Reichstag building") on 9 November 1918.

Many Germans wanted an end to the war and increasing numbers began to associate with the political left, such as the [Social Democratic Party](/wiki/Social_Democratic_Party_of_Germany "Social Democratic Party of Germany") (SPD) and the more radical [Independent Social Democratic Party](/wiki/Independent_Social_Democratic_Party_of_Germany "Independent Social Democratic Party of Germany") (USPD), which demanded an end to the war. The [entry of the U.S.](/wiki/American_entry_into_World_War_I "American entry into World War I") into the war in April 1917 tipped the long-run [balance of power](/wiki/Balance_of_power_(international_relations) "Balance of power (international relations)") even more in favour of the Allies.

The end of October 1918, in [Kiel](/wiki/Kiel "Kiel"), in northern Germany, saw the beginning of the [German Revolution of 1918–1919](/wiki/German_Revolution_of_1918%E2%80%931919 "German Revolution of 1918–1919"). Units of the German Navy refused to set sail for a last, large-scale operation in a war which they saw as good as lost, initiating the uprising. On 3 November, the revolt spread to other cities and states of the country, in many of which [workers' and soldiers' councils](/wiki/German_workers%27_and_soldiers%27_councils_1918%E2%80%931919 "German workers' and soldiers' councils 1918–1919") were established. Meanwhile, Hindenburg and the senior generals lost confidence in the Kaiser and his government.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[Bulgaria](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Bulgaria "Kingdom of Bulgaria") signed the [Armistice of Salonica](/wiki/Armistice_of_Salonica "Armistice of Salonica") on 29 September 1918. The [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire") signed the [Armistice of Mudros](/wiki/Armistice_of_Mudros "Armistice of Mudros") on 30 October 1918. Between 24 October and 3 November 1918, Italy defeated [Austria-Hungary](/wiki/Austria-Hungary "Austria-Hungary") in the [battle of Vittorio Veneto](/wiki/Battle_of_Vittorio_Veneto "Battle of Vittorio Veneto"), which forced Austria-Hungary to sign the [Armistice of Villa Giusti](/wiki/Armistice_of_Villa_Giusti "Armistice of Villa Giusti") on 3 November 1918. So, in November 1918, with internal revolution, the Allies [advancing toward Germany on the Western Front](/wiki/Hundred_Days_Offensive "Hundred Days Offensive"), Austria-Hungary falling apart from multiple ethnic tensions, its other allies out of the war and pressure from the German high command, the Kaiser and all German ruling kings, dukes, and princes abdicated, and [German nobility](/wiki/German_nobility "German nobility") was abolished. On 9 November, the Social Democrat [Philipp Scheidemann](/wiki/Philipp_Scheidemann "Philipp Scheidemann") [proclaimed a republic](/wiki/Proclamation_of_the_republic_in_Germany "Proclamation of the republic in Germany"). The new government led by the [German Social Democrats](/wiki/Social_Democratic_Party_of_Germany "Social Democratic Party of Germany") called for and received an [armistice](/wiki/Armistice_of_11_November_1918 "Armistice of 11 November 1918") on 11 November. It was succeeded by the [Weimar Republic](/wiki/Weimar_Republic "Weimar Republic").[[112]](#cite_note-114) Those opposed, including disaffected veterans, joined a diverse set of paramilitary and underground political groups such as the [Freikorps](/wiki/Freikorps "Freikorps"), the [Organisation Consul](/wiki/Organisation_Consul "Organisation Consul"), and the Communists.

## Constitution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=German_Empire&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Constitution")]

Main article: [Constitution of the German Empire](/wiki/Constitution_of_the_German_Empire "Constitution of the German Empire")

The Empire was a [federal](/wiki/Federal_monarchy "Federal monarchy") [parliamentary](/wiki/Parliamentary_system "Parliamentary system") [constitutional monarchy](/wiki/Constitutional_monarchy "Constitutional monarchy").

The [Federal Council](/wiki/Bundesrat_(German_Empire) "Bundesrat (German Empire)") (*Bundesrat*) held sovereignty over the Empire and served as its highest authority.[[113]](#cite_note-115) The *Bundesrat* was a legislative body that possessed the right of [legislative initiative](/wiki/Right_of_initiative_(legislative) "Right of initiative (legislative)") (Article VII Nr. 1) and, because all laws required its consent, could effectively veto any bill coming from the *Reichstag* (Article V).[[114]](#cite_note-Constitution_of_the_German_Empire-116) The *Bundesrat* was able to set guidelines and make organisational changes within the executive branch, act as supreme arbitrator in administrative disputes between states, and serve as constitutional court for states that did not have a constitutional court (Article LXXVI).[[114]](#cite_note-Constitution_of_the_German_Empire-116) It was composed of representatives who were appointed by and reported to the state governments.[[115]](#cite_note-117)

The [Imperial Diet](/wiki/Reichstag_(German_Empire) "Reichstag (German Empire)") (*Reichstag*) was a legislative body elected by universal male suffrage that effectively served as parliament. It had the right to propose bills and, with the concurrence of the *Bundesrat*, approve the state budget annually and the military budget for periods of seven years until 1893, then after that for five years. All laws required the *Reichstag's* approval to pass.[[116]](#cite_note-Bundeszentrale_für_politische_Bildung-2016-118) After the [constitutional reforms of October 1918](/wiki/German_constitutional_reforms_of_October_1918 "German constitutional reforms of October 1918"), the Reich chancellor, through a change to Article XV, became dependent on the confidence of the *Reichstag* rather than the emperor.[[117]](#cite_note-119)

The [emperor](/wiki/German_emperor "German emperor") (*[Kaiser](/wiki/Kaiser "Kaiser")*) was [head of state](/wiki/Head_of_state "Head of state") of the Empire – he was not a ruler. He appointed the chancellor, usually the person able to command the confidence of the *Reichstag*. The chancellor, in consultation with the emperor, determined the government's broad policy guidelines and presented them to the *Reichstag.*[[116]](#cite_note-Bundeszentrale_für_politische_Bildung-2016-118) On the advice of the chancellor, the emperor appointed the ministers and – at least formally – all other imperial officers. All acts of the emperor except for military directives[[118]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHuber19881003-120) required the countersignature of the chancellor (Article XVII). The emperor was also responsible for signing bills into law, declaring war (which required the consent of the *Bundesrat*), negotiating peace, making treaties, and calling and adjourning sessions of the *Bundesrat* and the *Reichstag* (Articles XI and XII). The emperor was [commander-in-chief](/wiki/Commander-in-chief "Commander-in-chief") of the Empire's [Army](/wiki/Imperial_German_Army "Imperial German Army") (Article LXIII) and [Navy](/wiki/Imperial_German_Navy "Imperial German Navy") (Article LIII);[[114]](#cite_note-Constitution_of_the_German_Empire-116) when exercising his military authority he had [plenary power](/wiki/Plenary_power "Plenary power").

The chancellor was [head of government](/wiki/Head_of_government "Head of government") and chaired the *Bundesrat* and the Imperial Government, led the lawmaking process and countersigned all acts of the emperor (except for military directives).[[116]](#cite_note-Bundeszentrale_für_politische_Bildung-2016-118)

## Constituent states

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=German_Empire&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Constituent states")]

Main article: [States of the German Empire](/wiki/States_of_the_German_Empire "States of the German Empire")

See also: [List of historic states of Germany](/wiki/List_of_historic_states_of_Germany "List of historic states of Germany")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1d/Wappen_und_Flaggen_des_Deutschen_Reichs_und_der_Preu%C3%9Fischen_Provinzen.png/330px-Wappen_und_Flaggen_des_Deutschen_Reichs_und_der_Preu%C3%9Fischen_Provinzen.png)](/wiki/File:Wappen_und_Flaggen_des_Deutschen_Reichs_und_der_Preu%C3%9Fischen_Provinzen.png)

Coats of arms and flags of the constituent states in 1900

The [Constitution of the German Empire](/wiki/Constitution_of_the_German_Empire "Constitution of the German Empire") came into force on 4 May 1871 and united 25 constituent states. These states consisted of four kingdoms, six grand duchies, five duchies, seven principalities and three free [Hanseatic](/wiki/Hanseaten_(class) "Hanseaten (class)") cities.[[119]](#cite_note-121) In addition, the recently annexed area of [Alsace-Lorraine](/wiki/Alsace-Lorraine "Alsace-Lorraine") was not a state in its own right, but was designated as a *Reichsland*, or imperial territory by a law of 25 June 1873.[[120]](#cite_note-122)

The Empire was constituted as a monarchy, as were nearly all of the states, with the exception of the three free cities which had a republican form of government. Several of these states had gained sovereignty following the dissolution of the [Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire") in 1806 and had been *[de facto](/wiki/De_facto "De facto")* sovereign from the mid-1600s onward. Others were created as sovereign states after the [Congress of Vienna](/wiki/Congress_of_Vienna "Congress of Vienna") in 1815. Prussia was the largest of the constituent states, comprising two-thirds of the Empire's territory. States were not necessarily contiguous– many existed in several parts with [exclaves](/wiki/Exclaves "Exclaves") and [enclaves](/wiki/Enclaves "Enclaves")– as a result of historical acquisitions or divisions, and in several cases, the extinction of the ruling dynasties.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Each component elected deputies to the *Reichstag* via single-member districts. Each state also sent appointed representatives to the *Bundesrat* to represent their interests, though this right was not extended to Alsace-Lorraine until 1911.[[121]](#cite_note-123)

Unusually for a federation or a nation-state, the German states maintained limited autonomy over foreign affairs and continued to exchange ambassadors and other diplomats, both with each other and directly with foreign nations. Shortly after the Empire was proclaimed, Bismarck implemented a convention in which his sovereign would only exchange envoys with other German states as the King of Prussia, while envoys to foreign nations always received credentials from the monarch in his capacity as German Emperor. In this way, the Prussian foreign ministry was largely tasked with managing relations with the other German states while the Imperial foreign ministry managed Germany's external relations.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Demographics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=German_Empire&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Demographics")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/85/Sprachen_Deutsches_Reich_1900.png/500px-Sprachen_Deutsches_Reich_1900.png)](/wiki/File:Sprachen_Deutsches_Reich_1900.png)

Percentage of linguistic minorities of the German Empire in 1900 by [Kreis](/wiki/Kreis_in_Prussia "Kreis in Prussia")

About 92% of the population spoke German as their first language. The only minority language with a significant number of speakers (5.4%) was [Polish](/wiki/Polish_language "Polish language") (a figure that rises to over 6% when including the related [Kashubian](/wiki/Kashubian_language "Kashubian language") and [Masurian](/wiki/Masurian_dialects "Masurian dialects") languages).

The non-German [Germanic languages](/wiki/Germanic_languages "Germanic languages") (0.5%), like [Danish](/wiki/Danish_language "Danish language"), [Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language") and [Frisian](/wiki/North_Frisian_language "North Frisian language"), were located in the north and northwest of the empire, near the borders with [Denmark](/wiki/Denmark "Denmark"), the [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands"), [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium"), and [Luxembourg](/wiki/Luxembourg "Luxembourg"). [Low German](/wiki/Low_German "Low German") was spoken throughout northern Germany and, though linguistically as distinct from High German (*Hochdeutsch*) as from Dutch and English, was considered "German", hence also its name. Danish and [Frisian](/wiki/North_Frisian_language "North Frisian language") were spoken predominantly in the north of the [Prussian](/wiki/Prussia "Prussia") [province of Schleswig-Holstein](/wiki/Province_of_Schleswig-Holstein "Province of Schleswig-Holstein") and Dutch in the western border areas of Prussia ([Hanover](/wiki/Province_of_Hanover "Province of Hanover"), [Westphalia](/wiki/Province_of_Westphalia "Province of Westphalia"), and the [Rhine Province](/wiki/Rhine_Province "Rhine Province")).

Polish and other [West Slavic languages](/wiki/West_Slavic_languages "West Slavic languages") (6.28%) were spoken chiefly in the east.[[c]](#cite_note-124)
A few (0.5%) spoke French, the vast majority of these in the Reichsland [Elsass-Lothringen](/wiki/Elsass-Lothringen "Elsass-Lothringen") where francophones formed 11.6% of the total population.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Immigration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=German_Empire&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Immigration")]

In the 1860s, Russia removed privileges for German emigrants and placed pressure on German immigrants to assimilate. The majority of German emigrants left Russia after the turn of the century. Many of these ethnic Germans immigrated back to Germany.[[123]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOltmer2006429–446-127)

## Religion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=German_Empire&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Religion")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/98/Wilhelm_II_Auguste_Viktoria_Jerusalem_1898.jpg/250px-Wilhelm_II_Auguste_Viktoria_Jerusalem_1898.jpg)](/wiki/File:Wilhelm_II_Auguste_Viktoria_Jerusalem_1898.jpg)

Emperor Wilhelm II, who was the *Supreme Governor of the [Evangelical Church of Prussia](/wiki/Evangelical_Church_of_Prussia "Evangelical Church of Prussia")'s older Provinces*, and Empress [Augusta Victoria](/wiki/Augusta_Viktoria_of_Schleswig-Holstein-Sonderburg-Augustenburg "Augusta Viktoria of Schleswig-Holstein-Sonderburg-Augustenburg") after the inauguration of the [Evangelical Church of the Redeemer](/wiki/Lutheran_Church_of_the_Redeemer,_Jerusalem "Lutheran Church of the Redeemer, Jerusalem") in Jerusalem ([Reformation Day](/wiki/Reformation_Day "Reformation Day"), 31 October 1898)

Further information: [Landeskirche](/wiki/Landeskirche "Landeskirche")

Generally, religious demographics of the [early modern period](/wiki/Early_modern_period "Early modern period") hardly changed. Still, there were almost entirely Catholic areas (Lower and Upper Bavaria, northern Westphalia, Upper Silesia, etc.) and almost entirely Protestant areas (Schleswig-Holstein, Pomerania, Saxony, etc.). Confessional prejudices, especially towards mixed marriages, were still common. Bit by bit, through internal migration, religious blending was more and more common. In eastern territories, confession was almost uniquely perceived to be connected to one's ethnicity and the equation "Protestant = German, Catholic = Polish" was held to be valid. In areas affected by immigration in the Ruhr area and Westphalia, as well as in some large cities, the religious landscape changed substantially. This was especially true in largely Catholic areas of Westphalia, which changed through Protestant immigration from the eastern provinces.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Politically, the confessional division of Germany had considerable consequences. In Catholic areas, the Centre Party had a big electorate. On the other hand, Social Democrats and [Free Trade Unions](/wiki/Free_Trade_Unions_(Germany) "Free Trade Unions (Germany)") usually received hardly any votes in the Catholic areas of the Ruhr. This began to change with the secularization arising in the last decades of the German Empire.

| **Total** | 28,331,152 | 62.63 | 16,232,651 | 35.89 | 78,031 | 0.17 | 561,612 | 1.24 | 30,615 | 0.07 |

In Germany's [overseas colonial empire](/wiki/German_colonial_empire "German colonial empire"), millions of subjects practiced various [indigenous religions](/wiki/Indigenous_religions "Indigenous religions") in addition to Christianity. Over two million [Muslims](/wiki/Muslims "Muslims") also lived under German colonial rule, primarily in [German East Africa](/wiki/German_East_Africa "German East Africa").[[124]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELevenda201464-128)

## Legacy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=German_Empire&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: Legacy")]

The defeat and [aftermath of the First World War](/wiki/Aftermath_of_the_First_World_War "Aftermath of the First World War") and the penalties imposed by the [Treaty of Versailles](/wiki/Treaty_of_Versailles "Treaty of Versailles") shaped the positive memory of the Empire, especially among Germans who distrusted and despised the Weimar Republic. Conservatives, liberals, socialists, nationalists, Catholics and Protestants all had their own interpretations, which led to a fractious political and social climate in Germany in the aftermath of the empire's collapse.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c9/War_Ensign_of_Germany_%281903%E2%80%931919%29.svg/250px-War_Ensign_of_Germany_%281903%E2%80%931919%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:War_Ensign_of_Germany_(1903%E2%80%931919).svg)

[War flag](/wiki/Reichskriegsflagge "Reichskriegsflagge") of the German Empire used since 1903. In 1956, the [Iron Cross](/wiki/Iron_Cross "Iron Cross") was re-introduced as the symbol of the **[Bundeswehr](/wiki/Bundeswehr "Bundeswehr")**, the modern German armed forces.

Under Bismarck, a united German state had finally been achieved, but it remained a Prussian-dominated state and did not include German Austria as [Pan-German](/wiki/Pan-German "Pan-German") nationalists had desired. The influence of Prussian [militarism](/wiki/Militarism "Militarism"), the Empire's colonial efforts and its vigorous, competitive industrial prowess all gained it the dislike and envy of other nations. The German Empire enacted a number of progressive reforms, such as Europe's first social welfare system and freedom of press. There was also a modern system for electing the federal parliament, the Reichstag, in which every adult man had one vote. This enabled the [Social Democrats](/wiki/Social_Democratic_Party_of_Germany "Social Democratic Party of Germany") and the Catholic [Centre Party](/wiki/Centre_Party_(Germany) "Centre Party (Germany)") to play considerable roles in the empire's political life despite the continued hostility of Prussian aristocrats.

The era of the German Empire is well remembered in Germany as one of great cultural and intellectual vigour. [Thomas Mann](/wiki/Thomas_Mann "Thomas Mann") published his novel *[Buddenbrooks](/wiki/Buddenbrooks "Buddenbrooks")* in 1901. [Theodor Mommsen](/wiki/Theodor_Mommsen "Theodor Mommsen") received the [Nobel prize for literature](/wiki/Nobel_prize_for_literature "Nobel prize for literature") a year later for his Roman history. Painters like the groups [Der Blaue Reiter](/wiki/Der_Blaue_Reiter "Der Blaue Reiter") and [Die Brücke](/wiki/Die_Br%C3%BCcke "Die Brücke") made a significant contribution to modern art. The [AEG turbine factory](/wiki/AEG_turbine_factory "AEG turbine factory") in Berlin by [Peter Behrens](/wiki/Peter_Behrens "Peter Behrens") from 1909 was a milestone in classic modern architecture and an outstanding example of emerging functionalism. The social, economic, and scientific successes of this *[Gründerzeit](/wiki/Gr%C3%BCnderzeit "Gründerzeit")*, or founding epoch, have sometimes led the Wilhelmine era to be regarded as a [golden age](/wiki/Golden_age "Golden age").

In the field of economics, the "*Kaiserzeit*" laid the foundation of Germany's status as one of the world's leading economic powers. The iron and coal industries of the [Ruhr](/wiki/Ruhr "Ruhr"), the [Saar](/wiki/Saarland "Saarland") and [Upper Silesia](/wiki/Upper_Silesia "Upper Silesia") especially contributed to that process. The first motorcar was built by [Karl Benz](/wiki/Karl_Benz "Karl Benz") in 1886. The enormous growth of industrial production and industrial potential also led to a rapid urbanisation of Germany, which turned the Germans into a nation of city dwellers. More than 5 million people left Germany for the [United States](/wiki/German_American "German American") during the 19th century.[[125]](#cite_note-129)

### Sonderweg

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=German_Empire&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: Sonderweg")]

Main article: [Sonderweg](/wiki/Sonderweg "Sonderweg")

Many historians have emphasized the central importance of a German *[Sonderweg](/wiki/Sonderweg "Sonderweg")* or "special path" (or "exceptionalism") as the root of Nazism and the German catastrophe in the 20th century. According to the 1988 historiography by [Jürgen Kocka](/wiki/J%C3%BCrgen_Kocka "Jürgen Kocka"), the process of nation-building from above had very grievous long-term implications. In terms of parliamentary democracy, Parliament was kept weak, the parties were fragmented, and there was a high level of mutual distrust. The Nazis built on the illiberal, anti-pluralist elements of Weimar's political culture. The Junker elites (the large landowners in the east) and senior civil servants used their great power and influence well into the twentieth century to frustrate any movement toward democracy. They played an especially negative role in the crisis of 1930–1933. Bismarck's emphasis on military force amplified the voice of the officer corps, which combined advanced modernisation of military technology with reactionary politics. The rising upper-middle-class elites, in the business, financial and professional worlds, tended to accept the values of the old traditional elites. The German Empire was for [Hans-Ulrich Wehler](/wiki/Hans-Ulrich_Wehler "Hans-Ulrich Wehler") a strange mixture of highly successful capitalist industrialisation and socio-economic modernisation on the one hand, and of surviving pre-industrial institutions, power relations and traditional cultures on the other. Wehler argues that it produced a high degree of internal tension, which led on the one hand to the suppression of socialists, Catholics and reformers, and on the other hand to a highly aggressive foreign policy. For these reasons [Fritz Fischer](/wiki/Fritz_Fischer_(historian) "Fritz Fischer (historian)") and his students emphasised Germany's primary guilt for causing the First World War.[[126]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKocka19883–16-130)

Wehler, a leader of the [Bielefeld School](/wiki/Bielefeld_School "Bielefeld School") of social history, places the origins of Germany's path to disaster in the 1860s–1870s, when economic modernisation took place, but political modernisation did not happen, and the old Prussian rural elite remained in firm control of the army, diplomacy and the civil service. Traditional, aristocratic, premodern society battled an emerging capitalist, bourgeois, modernising society. Recognising the importance of modernising forces in industry and the economy and in the cultural realm, Wehler argues that reactionary traditionalism dominated the political hierarchy of power in Germany, as well as social mentalities and in class relations (*Klassenhabitus*). The catastrophic German politics between 1914 and 1945 are interpreted in terms of a delayed modernisation of its political structures. At the core of Wehler's interpretation is his treatment of "the middle class" and "revolution", each of which was instrumental in shaping the 20th century. Wehler's examination of Nazi rule is shaped by his concept of "charismatic domination", which focuses heavily on Hitler.[[127]](#cite_note-131)

The historiographical concept of a German *Sonderweg* has had a turbulent history. 19th-century scholars who emphasised a separate German path to modernity saw it as a positive factor that differentiated Germany from the "western path" typified by Great Britain. They stressed the strong bureaucratic state, reforms initiated by Bismarck and other strong leaders, the Prussian service ethos, the high culture of philosophy and music, and Germany's pioneering of a social welfare state. In the 1950s, historians in [West Germany](/wiki/West_Germany "West Germany") argued that the *Sonderweg* led Germany to the disaster of 1933–1945. The special circumstances of German historical structures and experiences, were interpreted as preconditions that, while not directly causing National Socialism, did hamper the development of a liberal democracy and facilitate the rise of fascism. The *Sonderweg* paradigm has provided the impetus for at least three strands of research in German historiography: the "[long 19th century](/wiki/Long_19th_century "Long 19th century")", the history of the bourgeoisie, and comparisons with the West. After 1990, increased attention to cultural dimensions and to comparative and relational history moved German historiography to different topics, with much less attention paid to the *Sonderweg*. While some historians have abandoned the *Sonderweg* thesis, they have not provided a generally accepted alternative interpretation.[[128]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESmith2008225–240-132)

## Military

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=German_Empire&action=edit&section=45 "Edit section: Military")]

The Empire of Germany had two armed forces:

* the [Imperial German Army](/wiki/Imperial_German_Army "Imperial German Army"), which included
  + *[Schutztruppe](/wiki/Schutztruppe "Schutztruppe")*,
  + [Imperial German Air Service](/wiki/Imperial_German_Air_Service "Imperial German Air Service"), and
* the [Imperial German Navy](/wiki/Imperial_German_Navy "Imperial German Navy"),
  + *[Seebataillon](/wiki/Seebataillon "Seebataillon")*.

## Territorial legacy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=German_Empire&action=edit&section=46 "Edit section: Territorial legacy")]

Main article: [Territorial evolution of Germany](/wiki/Territorial_evolution_of_Germany "Territorial evolution of Germany")

In addition to present-day Germany, large parts of what comprised the German Empire now belong to several other modern European countries.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/75/Former_German_territories.svg/500px-Former_German_territories.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Former_German_territories.svg)

German territories lost in both World Wars are shown in black, while present-day Germany is marked dark grey on this 1914 map.

| When lost from Germany | Name | Country | Region |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Both World Wars | [Alsace-Lorraine](/wiki/Alsace-Lorraine "Alsace-Lorraine") | France | The [departments](/wiki/Departments_of_France "Departments of France") of [Bas-Rhin](/wiki/Bas-Rhin "Bas-Rhin"), [Haut-Rhin](/wiki/Haut-Rhin "Haut-Rhin") (both within [Alsace](/wiki/Alsace "Alsace") region) and [Moselle](/wiki/Moselle_(department) "Moselle (department)") (northeastern part of the [Lorraine](/wiki/Lorraine "Lorraine") region) |
| Both World Wars | [Eupen-Malmedy](/wiki/Eupen-Malmedy "Eupen-Malmedy") | [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium") | The two towns of [Eupen](/wiki/Eupen "Eupen") and [Malmedy](/wiki/Malmedy "Malmedy") and the [municipalities](/wiki/Municipalities_of_Belgium "Municipalities of Belgium") of [Amel](/wiki/Amel "Amel"), [Büllingen](/wiki/B%C3%BCllingen "Büllingen"), [Burg-Reuland](/wiki/Burg-Reuland "Burg-Reuland"), [Bütgenbach](/wiki/B%C3%BCtgenbach "Bütgenbach"), [Kelmis](/wiki/Kelmis "Kelmis"), [Lontzen](/wiki/Lontzen "Lontzen"), [Raeren](/wiki/Raeren "Raeren"), [Waimes](/wiki/Waimes "Waimes") and [St. Vith](/wiki/St._Vith "St. Vith") (all are parts of [Liège Province](/wiki/Li%C3%A8ge_Province "Liège Province") in the [Wallonia](/wiki/Wallonia "Wallonia") region on the Belgian–German border) |
| World War II | [Wylerberg](/wiki/Duivelsberg "Duivelsberg") | [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands") | [Duivelsberg](/wiki/Duivelsberg "Duivelsberg") ([German](/wiki/German_language "German language"): Wylerberg), an uninhabited hill and some nearby slivers of land, annexed by the Netherlands after WWII |
| World War I | [Northern Schleswig](/wiki/South_Jutland_County "South Jutland County") | [Denmark](/wiki/Denmark "Denmark") | [South Jutland County](/wiki/South_Jutland_County "South Jutland County") (excluding towns of Taps, Hejle and Vejstrup), and the towns of Hviding, Roager and Spandet |
| Both World Wars | [Hultschin Region](/wiki/Hlu%C4%8D%C3%ADn_Region "Hlučín Region") | [Czech Republic](/wiki/Czech_Republic "Czech Republic") | [Hlučín Region](/wiki/Hlu%C4%8D%C3%ADn_Region "Hlučín Region"), on the Czech–Polish border in Silesia, whose German population was [partially deported following WWII](/wiki/Flight_and_expulsion_of_Germans_(1944%E2%80%931950) "Flight and expulsion of Germans (1944–1950)"); part of [Czechoslovakia](/wiki/Czechoslovakia "Czechoslovakia") until its dissolution in 1992 |
| Both World Wars | [Memel Territory](/wiki/Klaip%C4%97da_Region "Klaipėda Region") | [Lithuania](/wiki/Lithuania "Lithuania") | [Klaipėda Region](/wiki/Klaip%C4%97da_Region "Klaipėda Region"), annexed by [Lithuania](/wiki/Lithuania "Lithuania") in 1923, retaken by [Nazi Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany") in 1939, from which Germans were [deported following WWII](/wiki/Flight_and_expulsion_of_Germans_(1944%E2%80%931950) "Flight and expulsion of Germans (1944–1950)") by the Soviet authorities; now part of independent Lithuania since 1990 |
| Both World Wars | Most of [West Prussia](/wiki/West_Prussia "West Prussia") and [Posen](/wiki/Province_of_Posen "Province of Posen"), a part of [Upper Silesia](/wiki/Upper_Silesia "Upper Silesia"), parts of the districts of [Bütow](/wiki/Byt%C3%B3w "Bytów") and [Lauenburg](/wiki/L%C4%99bork "Lębork") in [Pomerania](/wiki/Province_of_Pomerania_(1815%E2%80%931945) "Province of Pomerania (1815–1945)"),[[129]](#cite_note-Brockhaus_1933-133) [Soldau](/wiki/Dzia%C5%82dowo "Działdowo") in [East Prussia](/wiki/East_Prussia "East Prussia") | [Poland](/wiki/Poland "Poland") | [Silesian](/wiki/Silesian_Voivodeship "Silesian Voivodeship"), [Pomeranian](/wiki/Pomeranian_Voivodeship "Pomeranian Voivodeship") and [Greater Poland Voivodeships](/wiki/Greater_Poland_Voivodeship "Greater Poland Voivodeship"), parts of [Bytów](/wiki/Byt%C3%B3w_County "Bytów County") and [Lębork](/wiki/L%C4%99bork_County "Lębork County") counties, and [Działdowo County](/wiki/Dzia%C5%82dowo_County "Działdowo County") with the town proper (the German population was [deported following WWII](/wiki/Flight_and_expulsion_of_Germans_(1944%E2%80%931950) "Flight and expulsion of Germans (1944–1950)")) |
| World War II | [Silesia](/wiki/Province_of_Silesia "Province of Silesia"), [East Brandenburg](/wiki/Neumark "Neumark"), [Warmia](/wiki/Warmia "Warmia"), [Masuria](/wiki/Masuria "Masuria"), southern East Prussia, central and eastern parts of [Pomerania](/wiki/Province_of_Pomerania_(1815%E2%80%931945) "Province of Pomerania (1815–1945)") | [Poland](/wiki/Poland "Poland") | Northern and western parts of the country, including [Pomerania](/wiki/Pomerania "Pomerania"), [Silesia](/wiki/Silesia "Silesia"), [Lubusz Land](/wiki/Lubusz_Land "Lubusz Land"), [Warmia and Masuria](/wiki/Warmian-Masurian_Voivodeship "Warmian-Masurian Voivodeship"), from all of which Germans were [deported following WWII](/wiki/Flight_and_expulsion_of_Germans_(1944%E2%80%931950) "Flight and expulsion of Germans (1944–1950)") |
| World War II | Northern [East Prussia](/wiki/East_Prussia "East Prussia") | Russia | The [Kaliningrad Oblast](/wiki/Kaliningrad_Oblast "Kaliningrad Oblast") exclave on the Baltic, from which Germans were [deported following WWII](/wiki/Flight_and_expulsion_of_Germans_(1944%E2%80%931950) "Flight and expulsion of Germans (1944–1950)"). Transferred to the [Russian SFSR](/wiki/Russian_Soviet_Federative_Socialist_Republic "Russian Soviet Federative Socialist Republic") and continued as a part of Russia following the [collapse of the Soviet Union](/wiki/Collapse_of_the_Soviet_Union "Collapse of the Soviet Union") |

1. **[^](#cite_ref-10)** Pronounced [[ˌdɔʏtʃəs ˈʁaɪç]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Standard_German "Help:IPA/Standard German") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:De-Deutsches_Reich.ogg "File:De-Deutsches Reich.ogg"); today often referred to as *Deutsches Kaiserreich*.
2. **[^](#cite_ref-14)** German: *Zweites Reich*
3. **[^](#cite_ref-124)** The Slavic speakers included [Polish](/wiki/Polish_language "Polish language"), [Masurian](/wiki/Masurian_dialects "Masurian dialects"), [Kashubian](/wiki/Kashubian_language "Kashubian language"), [Sorbian](/wiki/Sorbian_languages "Sorbian languages") and [Czech](/wiki/Czech_language "Czech language") were located in the east; Polish mainly in the [Prussian](/wiki/Prussia "Prussia") [provinces](/wiki/Provinces_of_Prussia "Provinces of Prussia") of [Posen](/wiki/Province_of_Posen "Province of Posen"), [West Prussia](/wiki/West_Prussia "West Prussia") and [Silesia](/wiki/Province_of_Silesia "Province of Silesia") ([Upper Silesia](/wiki/Upper_Silesia "Upper Silesia")). Small islands also existed in [Recklinghausen](/wiki/Recklinghausen "Recklinghausen") ([Westphalia](/wiki/Province_of_Westphalia "Province of Westphalia")) with 13.8% of the population and in the [Kreis](/wiki/Districts_of_Prussia "Districts of Prussia") of [Calau](/wiki/Calau "Calau") ([Brandenburg](/wiki/Province_of_Brandenburg "Province of Brandenburg")) (5.5%) and in parts of [East Prussia](/wiki/East_Prussia "East Prussia") and [Pomerania](/wiki/Province_of_Pomerania_(1815%E2%80%931945) "Province of Pomerania (1815–1945)"). Czech was spoken predominantly in the south of the [Silesia](/wiki/Province_of_Silesia "Province of Silesia"), Masurian in the south of East Prussia, Kashubian in the north of West Prussia and Sorbian in the [Lusatian](/wiki/Lusatia "Lusatia") regions of Prussia (Brandenburg and Silesia) and the [Kingdom of Saxony](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Saxony "Kingdom of Saxony").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
4. **[^](#cite_ref-126)** Including [Low German](/wiki/Low_German "Low German")
